cases.title: Mixed HAGMA and metabolic alkalosis
cases.clinical_stem: 33M in ED with several days of prolonged vomiting and poor intake, now looking unwell and still unable to keep anything down. Mild epigastric discomfort, otherwise no clear focal complaint.

questions_flow.label: Primary acid-base disorder
questions_flow.prompt: What is the primary acid-base disorder?

questions_flow.label: Anion gap
questions_flow.prompt: What is the anion gap status?

questions_flow.label: Additional metabolic disorder
questions_flow.prompt: Is an additional metabolic disorder present?

options: Mixed high anion gap metabolic acidosis and metabolic alkalosis

answer_key.final_diagnosis: Mixed high anion gap metabolic acidosis and metabolic alkalosis

cases.learning_objective: Recognise a mixed high anion gap metabolic acidosis with concurrent metabolic alkalosis

explanation_blueprint.title: pH Status
explanation_blueprint.body: The pH is 7.36, which is near normal - do not let a near-normal pH hide an underlying process.

explanation_blueprint.title: Primary Disorder
explanation_blueprint.body: HCO3 is 23 mmol/L, which is low and supports a primary metabolic acidosis.

explanation_blueprint.title: Diagnosis
explanation_blueprint.body: The overall pattern is a mixed high anion gap metabolic acidosis with concurrent metabolic alkalosis.

explanation_blueprint.title: Compensation
explanation_blueprint.body: For winter, expected PaCO2 is about 42.5 mmHg (acceptable range 40.5-44.5), and the measured value is 41.7 mmHg, so the compensation is appropriate.

explanation_blueprint.title: Anion Gap Analysis
explanation_blueprint.body: The Anion gap is 140 - (96 + 23) = 21.0, which is raised. A raised anion gap suggests an additional unmeasured acid load. Consider causes such as lactate, ketones, or toxins.

explanation_blueprint.title: Additional Metabolic Process
explanation_blueprint.body: There is an additional metabolic process present: Metabolic alkalosis. This suggests a mixed disorder beyond simple compensation.

explanation_blueprint.title: Clinical Significance
explanation_blueprint.body: This mixed metabolic picture reflects multiple underlying processes, such as acid generation combined with vomiting or diuretic use. The presence of more than one disturbance should prompt a broader clinical search rather than a single unifying diagnosis.

explanation_blueprint.title: Key Takeaway
explanation_blueprint.body: A high anion gap acidosis with a higher-than-expected bicarbonate indicates a concurrent alkalosis. When the numbers don’t align, there is always another process.

cases.title: Lactic acidosis (sepsis)
cases.clinical_stem: 72M brought in by ambulance with abdominal pain and hypotension, now tachycardic and cold peripheries. He looks unwell and has been in shock since arrival.

questions_flow.label: Primary acid-base disorder
questions_flow.prompt: What is the primary acid-base disorder?

questions_flow.label: Anion gap
questions_flow.prompt: What is the anion gap status?

explanation_blueprint.title: pH Status
explanation_blueprint.body: The pH is < 7.35, consistent with an acidaemia.

explanation_blueprint.title: Primary Disorder
explanation_blueprint.body: HCO3 is 13.8 mmol/L, which is low and supports a primary metabolic acidosis.

explanation_blueprint.title: Diagnosis
explanation_blueprint.body: Lactate is 4.6 mmol/L, which supports lactic acidosis in the septic clinical context.

explanation_blueprint.title: Compensation
explanation_blueprint.body: For winter, expected PaCO2 is about 28.7 mmHg (acceptable range 26.7-30.7), and the measured value is 28.1 mmHg, so the compensation is appropriate.

explanation_blueprint.title: Anion Gap Analysis
explanation_blueprint.body: The Anion gap is 140 - (99 + 13.8) = 27.2, which is raised. A raised anion gap suggests an additional unmeasured acid load. Consider causes such as lactate, ketones, or toxins.

explanation_blueprint.title: Clinical Significance
explanation_blueprint.body: Lactic acidosis results from impaired tissue oxygen delivery or utilisation, leading to accumulation of lactate and a raised anion gap metabolic acidosis. Common causes include septic or cardiogenic shock, severe anaemia, or regional ischaemia. The elevated lactate reflects underlying hypoperfusion or cellular dysfunction. This is a marker of significant physiological stress, and urgent identification and treatment of the underlying cause is critical.

explanation_blueprint.title: Key Takeaway
explanation_blueprint.body: A raised lactate explains the gap, but the urgent job is still to find and reverse the cause of hypoperfusion or tissue stress.

cases.title: Simple respiratory acidosis
cases.clinical_stem: ED review of a 39-year-old woman noted to be drowsy with shallow respirations after taking sedating medications. ABG was sent on arrival.

questions_flow.label: Primary acid-base disorder
questions_flow.prompt: What is the primary acid-base disorder?

cases.learning_objective: Recognise acute respiratory acidosis with appropriate metabolic compensation

explanation_blueprint.title: pH Status
explanation_blueprint.body: The pH is < 7.35, consistent with an acidaemia.

explanation_blueprint.title: Primary Disorder
explanation_blueprint.body: PaCO2 is 56 mmHg, which is elevated and supports a primary respiratory acidosis.

explanation_blueprint.title: Diagnosis
explanation_blueprint.body: This pattern fits simple acute hypoventilation.

explanation_blueprint.title: Clinical Significance
explanation_blueprint.body: Respiratory acidosis is caused by hypoventilation, leading to retention of CO₂ and a fall in pH. This results in an elevated PaCO₂ with a compensatory rise in bicarbonate over time. Common causes include sedation, neuromuscular weakness, or airway obstruction. Recognising this pattern helps identify inadequate ventilation as the primary problem.

cases.title: Renal failure (uraemic HAGMA with appropriate respiratory compensation)
cases.clinical_stem: ED refers a 71-year-old woman with advanced kidney disease and reduced urine output over the last day. She’s been increasingly weak and nauseated, and is a bit drowsy on arrival.

questions_flow.label: Primary acid-base disorder
questions_flow.prompt: What is the primary acid-base disorder?

questions_flow.label: Anion gap
questions_flow.prompt: What is the anion gap status?

cases.learning_objective: Recognise high anion gap metabolic acidosis from uraemia with appropriate respiratory compensation

explanation_blueprint.title: pH Status
explanation_blueprint.body: The pH is < 7.35, consistent with an acidaemia.

explanation_blueprint.title: Primary Disorder
explanation_blueprint.body: HCO3 is 12 mmol/L, which is low and supports a primary metabolic acidosis.

explanation_blueprint.title: Diagnosis
explanation_blueprint.body: This pattern is most consistent with uraemic high anion gap metabolic acidosis from renal failure.

explanation_blueprint.title: Compensation
explanation_blueprint.body: For winter, expected PaCO2 is about 26.0 mmHg (acceptable range 24.0-28.0), and the measured value is 24.6 mmHg, so the compensation is appropriate.

explanation_blueprint.title: Anion Gap Analysis
explanation_blueprint.body: The Anion gap is 132 - (92 + 12) = 28.0, which is raised. A raised anion gap suggests an additional unmeasured acid load. Consider causes such as lactate, ketones, or toxins.

explanation_blueprint.title: Clinical Significance
explanation_blueprint.body: Uraemia causes a raised anion gap metabolic acidosis due to accumulation of unmeasured acids from impaired renal excretion. The anion gap rises as these acids accumulate, and bicarbonate falls accordingly. Respiratory compensation lowers PaCO₂, but the degree of acidosis reflects the severity of renal dysfunction.

explanation_blueprint.title: Key Takeaway
explanation_blueprint.body: Renal failure can explain a high anion gap, but compensation still needs to be checked before calling the picture isolated uraemia.

cases.title: Vomiting (metabolic alkalosis with respiratory compensation)
cases.clinical_stem: 35F in ED with persistent vomiting and a few days of retching, now complaining of nausea and a dry mouth. She looks a bit dehydrated and weak, with no diarrhoea or abdominal distension reported.

questions_flow.label: Primary acid-base disorder
questions_flow.prompt: What is the primary acid-base disorder?

cases.learning_objective: Recognise metabolic alkalosis with appropriate respiratory compensation

explanation_blueprint.title: pH Status
explanation_blueprint.body: The pH is > 7.45, consistent with an alkalaemia.

explanation_blueprint.title: Primary Disorder
explanation_blueprint.body: HCO3 is 40 mmol/L, which is elevated and supports a primary metabolic alkalosis.

explanation_blueprint.title: Diagnosis
explanation_blueprint.body: This pattern fits chloride-responsive metabolic alkalosis from gastric losses.

explanation_blueprint.title: Compensation
explanation_blueprint.body: For metabolic alkalosis compensation, expected PaCO2 is about 51.2 mmHg (acceptable range 48.2-54.2), and the measured value is 50.3 mmHg, so the compensation is appropriate.

explanation_blueprint.title: Clinical Significance
explanation_blueprint.body: Vomiting causes loss of gastric acid, leading to a metabolic alkalosis with elevated bicarbonate and increased pH. The body compensates by hypoventilating to retain CO₂, raising PaCO₂. Ongoing chloride and volume depletion help maintain the alkalosis, so recognising this pattern is important as correction requires replacement of both volume and chloride.

cases.title: Diuretic use (metabolic alkalosis with respiratory compensation)
cases.clinical_stem: 71F in ED with a few days of dizziness and fatigue after a recent increase in her thiazide. She looks a bit dry on exam and says she’s been passing more urine than usual.

questions_flow.label: Primary acid-base disorder
questions_flow.prompt: What is the primary acid-base disorder?

cases.learning_objective: Recognise chloride-responsive metabolic alkalosis from diuretic-associated volume depletion

explanation_blueprint.title: pH Status
explanation_blueprint.body: The pH is in the normal range (7.35 - 7.45).

explanation_blueprint.title: Primary Disorder
explanation_blueprint.body: HCO3 is 30 mmol/L, which is elevated and supports a primary metabolic alkalosis.

explanation_blueprint.title: Diagnosis
explanation_blueprint.body: This pattern fits chloride-responsive metabolic alkalosis. Diuretic use causes volume depletion and chloride loss, which maintains the alkalosis.

explanation_blueprint.title: Compensation
explanation_blueprint.body: For metabolic alkalosis compensation, expected PaCO2 is about 44.2 mmHg (acceptable range 41.2-47.2), and the measured value is 45.0 mmHg, so the compensation is appropriate.

explanation_blueprint.title: Clinical Significance
explanation_blueprint.body: Diuretic use can cause metabolic alkalosis through volume depletion and renal loss of chloride, leading to increased bicarbonate reabsorption. This results in a chloride-responsive metabolic alkalosis, often with low or low-normal potassium. The alkalosis is maintained by ongoing volume and chloride depletion, even after the initial trigger. Recognising this pattern is important, as correction of volume status and chloride deficit is key to reversing the disturbance.

cases.title: DKA with vomiting (mixed metabolic disorder)
cases.clinical_stem: 24-year-old woman with type 1 diabetes, missed her insulin for a couple of days and now has persistent vomiting with deep breathing. In ED she looks tachypnoeic and is still complaining of upper abdominal pain.

questions_flow.label: Primary acid-base disorder
questions_flow.prompt: What is the primary acid-base disorder?

questions_flow.label: Anion gap
questions_flow.prompt: What is the anion gap status?

questions_flow.label: Additional metabolic disorder
questions_flow.prompt: Is an additional metabolic disorder present?

explanation_blueprint.title: pH Status
explanation_blueprint.body: The pH is < 7.35, consistent with an acidaemia.

explanation_blueprint.title: Primary Disorder
explanation_blueprint.body: HCO3 is 13 mmol/L, which is low and supports a primary metabolic acidosis.

explanation_blueprint.title: Diagnosis
explanation_blueprint.body: This fits DKA with a concurrent metabolic alkalosis from vomiting rather than an isolated ketoacidosis.

explanation_blueprint.title: Compensation
explanation_blueprint.body: For winter, expected PaCO2 is about 27.5 mmHg (acceptable range 25.5-29.5), and the measured value is 28.0 mmHg, so the compensation is appropriate.

explanation_blueprint.title: Anion Gap Analysis
explanation_blueprint.body: The Anion gap is 136 - (91 + 13) = 32.0, which is raised. A raised anion gap suggests an additional unmeasured acid load. Consider causes such as lactate, ketones, or toxins.

explanation_blueprint.title: Additional Metabolic Process
explanation_blueprint.body: There is an additional metabolic process present: Metabolic alkalosis. This suggests a mixed disorder beyond simple compensation.

explanation_blueprint.title: Clinical Significance
explanation_blueprint.body: Insulin deficiency drives ketone production, causing a raised anion gap metabolic acidosis consistent with DKA. However, concurrent vomiting leads to loss of gastric acid and a superimposed metabolic alkalosis, which can partially offset the acidaemia and result in a higher-than-expected bicarbonate. This mixed picture may make the acidosis appear less severe than expected for the degree of hyperglycaemia.

explanation_blueprint.title: Key Takeaway
explanation_blueprint.body: A preserved bicarbonate in DKA should prompt a search for a concurrent alkalosis rather than reassurance that the acidosis is mild.

cases.title: COPD exacerbation (acute-on-chronic respiratory acidosis)
cases.clinical_stem: 65M with COPD, smoker, brought to ED with worsening breathlessness and a productive cough over the last few days. He’s more drowsy than usual and using accessory muscles.

questions_flow.label: Primary acid-base disorder
questions_flow.prompt: What is the primary acid-base disorder?

explanation_blueprint.title: pH Status
explanation_blueprint.body: The pH is < 7.35, consistent with an acidaemia.

explanation_blueprint.title: Primary Disorder
explanation_blueprint.body: PaCO2 is 72.5 mmHg, which is elevated and supports a primary respiratory acidosis.

explanation_blueprint.title: Diagnosis
explanation_blueprint.body: This pattern is consistent with an acute on chronic respiratory acidosis. Chronic CO2 retention leads to metabolic compensation, with an acute rise in the PaCO2 during exacerbation.

explanation_blueprint.title: Compensation
explanation_blueprint.body: The measured compensatory response does not fit the expected range for chronic respiratory acidosis, so the compensation is inappropriate.

explanation_blueprint.title: Clinical Significance
explanation_blueprint.body: An acute COPD exacerbation causes worsening ventilatory failure, leading to a rise in PaCO₂ and a fall in pH. Renal compensation has not had time to adjust, so the bicarbonate does not increase appropriately. This represents an acute deterioration on a background of chronic respiratory disease.

cases.title: Toxic alcohol (HAGMA with appropriate respiratory compensation)
cases.clinical_stem: 41M brought to ED with confusion and blurred vision after a possible ingestion. Family found a suspicious solvent container at home, and he also reports some epigastric pain.

questions_flow.label: Primary acid-base disorder
questions_flow.prompt: What is the primary acid-base disorder?

questions_flow.label: Anion gap
questions_flow.prompt: What is the anion gap status?

cases.learning_objective: Recognise toxic alcohol ingestion as a high anion gap metabolic acidosis with appropriate respiratory compensation

explanation_blueprint.title: pH Status
explanation_blueprint.body: The pH is < 7.35, consistent with an acidaemia.

explanation_blueprint.title: Primary Disorder
explanation_blueprint.body: HCO3 is 12 mmol/L, which is low and supports a primary metabolic acidosis.

explanation_blueprint.title: Diagnosis
explanation_blueprint.body: The clinical context and only modest lactate of 3.0 mmol/L support toxic alcohol ingestion over isolated lactic acidosis.

explanation_blueprint.title: Compensation
explanation_blueprint.body: For winter, expected PaCO2 is about 26.0 mmHg (acceptable range 24.0-28.0), and the measured value is 25.1 mmHg, so the compensation is appropriate.

explanation_blueprint.title: Anion Gap Analysis
explanation_blueprint.body: The Anion gap is 139 - (99 + 12) = 28.0, which is raised. A raised anion gap suggests an additional unmeasured acid load. Consider causes such as lactate, ketones, or toxins.

explanation_blueprint.title: Clinical Significance
explanation_blueprint.body: Toxic alcohol ingestion can cause a severe raised anion gap metabolic acidosis due to accumulation of acidic metabolites. Common agents include methanol and ethylene glycol. The degree of acidosis is often disproportionate to any measured lactate, and may worsen rapidly as metabolism progresses. The key clinical clue is an unexplained high anion gap acidosis without a clear metabolic cause. This is a time-critical diagnosis, and early recognition is essential to prevent ongoing toxicity and organ injury.

cases.title: Diuretic use (metabolic alkalosis with respiratory compensation)
cases.clinical_stem: 76M in ED with dizziness and fatigue after a recent increase in his thiazide dose. Mildly dry on exam, no vomiting reported.

questions_flow.label: Primary acid-base disorder
questions_flow.prompt: What is the primary acid-base disorder?

cases.learning_objective: Recognise chloride-responsive metabolic alkalosis from diuretic-associated volume depletion

explanation_blueprint.title: pH Status
explanation_blueprint.body: The pH is > 7.45, consistent with an alkalaemia.

explanation_blueprint.title: Primary Disorder
explanation_blueprint.body: HCO3 is 32 mmol/L, which is elevated and supports a primary metabolic alkalosis.

explanation_blueprint.title: Diagnosis
explanation_blueprint.body: This pattern fits chloride-responsive metabolic alkalosis. Diuretic use causes volume depletion and chloride loss, which maintains the alkalosis.

explanation_blueprint.title: Compensation
explanation_blueprint.body: For metabolic alkalosis compensation, expected PaCO2 is about 45.6 mmHg (acceptable range 42.6-48.6), and the measured value is 46.1 mmHg, so the compensation is appropriate.

explanation_blueprint.title: Clinical Significance
explanation_blueprint.body: Diuretic use can cause metabolic alkalosis through volume depletion and renal loss of chloride, leading to increased bicarbonate reabsorption. This results in a chloride-responsive metabolic alkalosis, often with low or low-normal potassium. The alkalosis is maintained by ongoing volume and chloride depletion, even after the initial trigger. Recognising this pattern is important, as correction of volume status and chloride deficit is key to reversing the disturbance.

cases.title: Lactic acidosis (sepsis)
cases.clinical_stem: 65F brought in by ambulance from home, hypotensive and tachycardic in ED. She’s had abdominal pain since overnight and looks in shock.

questions_flow.label: Primary acid-base disorder
questions_flow.prompt: What is the primary acid-base disorder?

questions_flow.label: Anion gap

explanation_blueprint.title: pH Status
explanation_blueprint.body: The pH is < 7.35, consistent with an acidaemia.

explanation_blueprint.title: Primary Disorder
explanation_blueprint.body: HCO3 is 13.0 mmol/L, which is low and supports a primary metabolic acidosis.

explanation_blueprint.title: Diagnosis
explanation_blueprint.body: Lactate is 7.5 mmol/L, which supports lactic acidosis in the septic clinical context.

explanation_blueprint.title: Compensation
explanation_blueprint.body: For winter, expected PaCO2 is about 27.5 mmHg (acceptable range 25.5-29.5), and the measured value is 28.0 mmHg, so the compensation is appropriate.

explanation_blueprint.title: Anion Gap Analysis
explanation_blueprint.body: The Anion gap is 140 - (96 + 13.0) = 31.0, which is raised. A raised anion gap suggests an additional unmeasured acid load. Consider causes such as lactate, ketones, or toxins.

explanation_blueprint.title: Clinical Significance
explanation_blueprint.body: Lactic acidosis results from impaired tissue oxygen delivery or utilisation, leading to accumulation of lactate and a raised anion gap metabolic acidosis. Common causes include septic or cardiogenic shock, severe anaemia, or regional ischaemia. The elevated lactate reflects underlying hypoperfusion or cellular dysfunction. This is a marker of significant physiological stress, and urgent identification and treatment of the underlying cause is critical.

explanation_blueprint.title: Key Takeaway
explanation_blueprint.body: A raised lactate explains the gap, but the urgent job is still to find and reverse the cause of hypoperfusion or tissue stress.

explanation_blueprint.body: HCO3 is 17 mmol/L, which is low and supports a primary metabolic acidosis.

explanation_blueprint.body: This pattern is most consistent with uraemic high anion gap metabolic acidosis from renal failure.

explanation_blueprint.body: For winter, expected PaCO2 is about 33.5 mmHg (acceptable range 31.5-35.5), and the measured value is 34.6 mmHg, so the compensation is appropriate.

explanation_blueprint.body: The Anion gap is 136 - (101 + 17) = 18.0, which is raised. A raised anion gap suggests an additional unmeasured acid load. Consider causes such as lactate, ketones, or toxins.

cases.learning_objective: Recognise the classic respiratory alkalosis plus high anion gap metabolic acidosis pattern of salicylate toxicity

explanation_blueprint.title: pH Status
explanation_blueprint.body: The pH is < 7.35, consistent with an acidaemia.

explanation_blueprint.title: Primary Disorder
explanation_blueprint.body: PaCO2 is 29.1 mmHg, which is low and supports a primary respiratory alkalosis.

explanation_blueprint.title: Diagnosis
explanation_blueprint.body: This is the classic salicylate pattern: respiratory alkalosis with concurrent high anion gap metabolic acidosis.

explanation_blueprint.title: Compensation
explanation_blueprint.body: If this were isolated acute respiratory alkalosis, expected HCO3 would be about 21.8 mmol/L (acceptable range 19.8-23.8), but the measured value is 15.0 mmol/L. That mismatch does not fit a single-process compensation pattern.

explanation_blueprint.title: Anion Gap Analysis
explanation_blueprint.body: The Anion gap is 141 - (99 + 15.0) = 27.0, which is raised. A raised anion gap suggests an additional unmeasured acid load. Consider causes such as lactate, ketones, or toxins.

explanation_blueprint.title: Additional Metabolic Process
explanation_blueprint.body: There is an additional metabolic process present: High anion gap metabolic acidosis. This suggests a mixed disorder beyond simple compensation.

explanation_blueprint.title: Clinical Significance
explanation_blueprint.body: This pattern is characteristic of salicylate toxicity, where early hyperventilation is followed by metabolic acidosis. The combination should prompt urgent consideration of overdose, even if the history is unclear. Early recognition is critical, as clinical deterioration can be rapid.

explanation_blueprint.title: Key Takeaway
explanation_blueprint.body: Salicylate cases are high-risk because the alkalosis and acidosis co-exist, so compensation formulas help expose the mixed picture quickly.

cases.title: COPD exacerbation (acute-on-chronic respiratory acidosis)
cases.clinical_stem: 68M with known COPD and a smoking history presents with worsening breathlessness over 3 days. He has a productive cough with increased sputum and is more drowsy than usual.

questions_flow.label: Primary acid-base disorder
questions_flow.prompt: What is the primary acid-base disorder?

explanation_blueprint.title: pH Status
explanation_blueprint.body: The pH is < 7.35, consistent with an acidaemia.

explanation_blueprint.title: Primary Disorder
explanation_blueprint.body: PaCO2 is 77.9 mmHg, which is elevated and supports a primary respiratory acidosis.

explanation_blueprint.title: Diagnosis
explanation_blueprint.body: This pattern is consistent with an acute on chronic respiratory acidosis. Chronic CO2 retention leads to metabolic compensation, with an acute rise in the PaCO2 during exacerbation.

explanation_blueprint.title: Compensation
explanation_blueprint.body: The measured compensatory response does not fit the expected range for chronic respiratory acidosis, so the compensation is inappropriate.

explanation_blueprint.title: Clinical Significance
explanation_blueprint.body: An acute COPD exacerbation causes worsening ventilatory failure, leading to a rise in PaCO₂ and a fall in pH. Renal compensation has not had time to adjust, so the bicarbonate does not increase appropriately. This represents an acute deterioration on a background of chronic respiratory disease.

cases.title: Lactic acidosis (sepsis)
cases.clinical_stem: 65M brought to ED with hypotension and tachycardia, drowsy but arousable. Cool peripheries and brief notes from triage suggest shock.

questions_flow.label: Primary acid-base disorder
questions_flow.prompt: What is the primary acid-base disorder?

questions_flow.label: Anion gap
questions_flow.prompt: What is the anion gap status?

explanation_blueprint.title: pH Status
explanation_blueprint.body: The pH is < 7.35, consistent with an acidaemia.

explanation_blueprint.title: Primary Disorder
explanation_blueprint.body: HCO3 is 15.6 mmol/L, which is low and supports a primary metabolic acidosis.

explanation_blueprint.title: Diagnosis
explanation_blueprint.body: Lactate is 4.7 mmol/L, which supports lactic acidosis in the septic clinical context.

explanation_blueprint.title: Compensation
explanation_blueprint.body: For winter, expected PaCO2 is about 31.4 mmHg (acceptable range 29.4-33.4), and the measured value is 33.2 mmHg, so the compensation is appropriate.

explanation_blueprint.title: Anion Gap Analysis
explanation_blueprint.body: The Anion gap is 141 - (95 + 15.6) = 30.4, which is raised. A raised anion gap suggests an additional unmeasured acid load. Consider causes such as lactate, ketones, or toxins.

explanation_blueprint.title: Clinical Significance
explanation_blueprint.body: Lactic acidosis results from impaired tissue oxygen delivery or utilisation, leading to accumulation of lactate and a raised anion gap metabolic acidosis. Common causes include septic or cardiogenic shock, severe anaemia, or regional ischaemia. The elevated lactate reflects underlying hypoperfusion or cellular dysfunction. This is a marker of significant physiological stress, and urgent identification and treatment of the underlying cause is critical.

explanation_blueprint.title: Key Takeaway
explanation_blueprint.body: A raised lactate explains the gap, but the urgent job is still to find and reverse the cause of hypoperfusion or tissue stress.

cases.title: Simple respiratory acidosis

explanation_blueprint.title: Diagnosis
explanation_blueprint.body: This pattern fits simple acute hypoventilation.

explanation_blueprint.title: Clinical Significance
explanation_blueprint.body: Respiratory acidosis is caused by hypoventilation, leading to retention of CO₂ and a fall in pH. This results in an elevated PaCO₂ with a compensatory rise in bicarbonate over time. Common causes include sedation, neuromuscular weakness, or airway obstruction. Recognising this pattern helps identify inadequate ventilation as the primary problem.

cases.title: Diuretic use (metabolic alkalosis with respiratory compensation)
cases.clinical_stem: 68F in ED with a few days of dizziness and fatigue after a recent thiazide dose increase. She looks a bit dry on exam and says she has been passing less urine than usual.

questions_flow.label: Primary acid-base disorder
questions_flow.prompt: What is the primary acid-base disorder?

cases.learning_objective: Recognise chloride-responsive metabolic alkalosis from diuretic-associated volume depletion

explanation_blueprint.title: pH Status
explanation_blueprint.body: The pH is > 7.45, consistent with an alkalaemia.

explanation_blueprint.title: Primary Disorder
explanation_blueprint.body: HCO3 is 32 mmol/L, which is elevated and supports a primary metabolic alkalosis.

explanation_blueprint.title: Diagnosis
explanation_blueprint.body: This pattern fits chloride-responsive metabolic alkalosis. Diuretic use causes volume depletion and chloride loss, which maintains the alkalosis.

explanation_blueprint.title: Compensation
explanation_blueprint.body: For metabolic alkalosis compensation, expected PaCO2 is about 45.6 mmHg (acceptable range 42.6-48.6), and the measured value is 45.1 mmHg, so the compensation is appropriate.

explanation_blueprint.title: Clinical Significance
explanation_blueprint.body: Diuretic use can cause metabolic alkalosis through volume depletion and renal loss of chloride, leading to increased bicarbonate reabsorption. This results in a chloride-responsive metabolic alkalosis, often with low or low-normal potassium. The alkalosis is maintained by ongoing volume and chloride depletion, even after the initial trigger. Recognising this pattern is important, as correction of volume status and chloride deficit is key to reversing the disturbance.

cases.title: Starvation ketosis (subtle HAGMA with appropriate respiratory compensation)
cases.clinical_stem: 58M in ED with 3/7 of poor oral intake and weight loss. He has felt fatigued, with some nausea and occasional vomiting.

questions_flow.label: Primary acid-base disorder
questions_flow.prompt: What is the primary acid-base disorder?

questions_flow.label: Anion gap
questions_flow.prompt: What is the anion gap status?

cases.learning_objective: Recognise starvation ketosis as a subtle high anion gap metabolic acidosis with appropriate respiratory compensation and normal-range glucose

explanation_blueprint.title: pH Status
explanation_blueprint.body: The pH is < 7.35, consistent with an acidaemia.

explanation_blueprint.title: Primary Disorder
explanation_blueprint.body: HCO3 is 18 mmol/L, which is low and supports a primary metabolic acidosis.

explanation_blueprint.title: Diagnosis
explanation_blueprint.body: The glucose is 5.3 mmol/L, which is not markedly elevated. This is more in keeping with starvation ketosis over DKA.

explanation_blueprint.title: Compensation
explanation_blueprint.body: For winter, expected PaCO2 is about 35.0 mmHg (acceptable range 33.0-37.0), and the measured value is 35.4 mmHg, so the compensation is appropriate.

explanation_blueprint.title: Anion Gap Analysis
explanation_blueprint.body: The Anion gap is 140 - (105 + 18) = 17.0, which is raised. A raised anion gap suggests an additional unmeasured acid load. Consider causes such as lactate, ketones, or toxins.

explanation_blueprint.title: Clinical Significance
explanation_blueprint.body: Increased ketone production in the setting of poor oral intake can cause a raised anion gap metabolic acidosis. This is typically milder than DKA because some endogenous insulin activity remains, limiting unchecked ketogenesis. The key clinical clue is a history of reduced intake with normal or only mildly elevated glucose. This pattern should prompt correction of the fasting state and assessment for the underlying reason the patient has not been eating.

explanation_blueprint.title: Key Takeaway
explanation_blueprint.body: When ketosis occurs without major hyperglycaemia, use the clinical context to separate starvation ketosis from DKA.

explanation_blueprint.title: pH Status
explanation_blueprint.body: The pH is < 7.35, consistent with an acidaemia.

explanation_blueprint.title: Primary Disorder
explanation_blueprint.body: PaCO2 is 62.7 mmHg, which is elevated and supports a primary respiratory acidosis.

explanation_blueprint.title: Diagnosis
explanation_blueprint.body: The overall pattern is respiratory acidosis with concurrent high anion gap metabolic acidosis.

explanation_blueprint.title: Compensation
explanation_blueprint.body: If this were isolated chronic respiratory acidosis, expected HCO3 would be about 33.1 mmol/L (acceptable range 31.1-35.1), but the measured value is 27.5 mmol/L. That mismatch does not fit a single-process compensation pattern.

explanation_blueprint.title: Anion Gap Analysis
explanation_blueprint.body: The Anion gap is 138 - (92 + 27.5) = 18.5, which is raised. A raised anion gap suggests an additional unmeasured acid load. Consider causes such as lactate, ketones, or toxins.

explanation_blueprint.title: Additional Metabolic Process
explanation_blueprint.body: There is an additional metabolic process present: High anion gap metabolic acidosis. This suggests a mixed disorder beyond simple compensation.

explanation_blueprint.title: Clinical Significance
explanation_blueprint.body: This pattern suggests severe physiological compromise, with both impaired ventilation and a concurrent metabolic acidosis. It is often seen in critically unwell patients and indicates a high risk of deterioration. Prompt assessment and escalation of care are essential.

explanation_blueprint.title: Key Takeaway
explanation_blueprint.body: When respiratory acidosis and a raised anion gap coexist, expected compensation helps confirm that two primary processes are present.

cases.title: Starvation ketosis (subtle HAGMA with appropriate respiratory compensation)
cases.clinical_stem: 22M in ED with 3/7 poor oral intake and recent weight loss, now c/o fatigue and weakness. He looks mildly dehydrated with dry mucous membranes.

cases.learning_objective: Recognise starvation ketosis as a subtle high anion gap metabolic acidosis with appropriate respiratory compensation and normal-range glucose

explanation_blueprint.title: pH Status
explanation_blueprint.body: The pH is < 7.35, consistent with an acidaemia.

explanation_blueprint.title: Primary Disorder
explanation_blueprint.body: HCO3 is 13 mmol/L, which is low and supports a primary metabolic acidosis.

explanation_blueprint.title: Diagnosis
explanation_blueprint.body: The glucose is 4.4 mmol/L, which is not markedly elevated. This is more in keeping with starvation ketosis over DKA.

explanation_blueprint.title: Compensation
explanation_blueprint.body: For winter, expected PaCO2 is about 27.5 mmHg (acceptable range 25.5-29.5), and the measured value is 27.2 mmHg, so the compensation is appropriate.

explanation_blueprint.title: Anion Gap Analysis
explanation_blueprint.body: The Anion gap is 138 - (103 + 13) = 22.0, which is raised. A raised anion gap suggests an additional unmeasured acid load. Consider causes such as lactate, ketones, or toxins.

explanation_blueprint.title: Clinical Significance
explanation_blueprint.body: Increased ketone production in the setting of poor oral intake can cause a raised anion gap metabolic acidosis. This is typically milder than DKA because some endogenous insulin activity remains, limiting unchecked ketogenesis. The key clinical clue is a history of reduced intake with normal or only mildly elevated glucose. This pattern should prompt correction of the fasting state and assessment for the underlying reason the patient has not been eating.

explanation_blueprint.title: Key Takeaway
explanation_blueprint.body: When ketosis occurs without major hyperglycaemia, use the clinical context to separate starvation ketosis from DKA.

cases.title: DKA (HAGMA with appropriate respiratory compensation)
cases.clinical_stem: 34F in ED with type 1 diabetes, says she missed insulin for the last day and has had polyuria since yesterday. She’s tachypnoeic with deep breathing on arrival.

cases.learning_objective: Recognise high anion gap metabolic acidosis with appropriate respiratory compensation

explanation_blueprint.title: pH Status
explanation_blueprint.body: The pH is < 7.35, consistent with an acidaemia.

explanation_blueprint.title: Primary Disorder
explanation_blueprint.body: HCO3 is 8 mmol/L, which is low and supports a primary metabolic acidosis.

explanation_blueprint.title: Diagnosis
explanation_blueprint.body: The glucose is 33.1 mmol/L, which strongly supports DKA as the cause of this high anion gap metabolic acidosis.

explanation_blueprint.title: Compensation
explanation_blueprint.body: For winter, expected PaCO2 is about 20.0 mmHg (acceptable range 18.0-22.0), and the measured value is 20.5 mmHg, so the compensation is appropriate.

explanation_blueprint.title: Anion Gap Analysis
explanation_blueprint.body: The Anion gap is 133 - (93 + 8) = 32.0, which is raised. A raised anion gap suggests an additional unmeasured acid load. Consider causes such as lactate, ketones, or toxins.

explanation_blueprint.title: Clinical Significance
explanation_blueprint.body: Insulin deficiency leads to unchecked lipolysis and ketone production, resulting in a raised anion gap metabolic acidosis. Marked hyperglycaemia is a key feature, distinguishing DKA from other causes of ketoacidosis. The acidosis is often more severe than in starvation or alcoholic ketoacidosis, and may be accompanied by dehydration and electrolyte disturbances.

explanation_blueprint.title: Key Takeaway
explanation_blueprint.body: High anion gap metabolic acidosis with marked hyperglycaemia suggests DKA, but always consider an additional metabolic process if compensation is inappropriate or the bicarbonate is not as expected.

explanation_blueprint.title: pH Status
explanation_blueprint.body: The pH is 7.35, which is near normal - do not let a near-normal pH hide an underlying process.

explanation_blueprint.title: Compensation
explanation_blueprint.body: For winter, expected PaCO2 is about 42.5 mmHg (acceptable range 40.5-44.5), and the measured value is 43.5 mmHg, so the compensation is appropriate.

explanation_blueprint.title: Anion Gap Analysis
explanation_blueprint.body: The Anion gap is 137 - (92 + 23) = 22.0, which is raised. A raised anion gap suggests an additional unmeasured acid load. Consider causes such as lactate, ketones, or toxins.

explanation_blueprint.body: A high anion gap acidosis with a higher-than-expected bicarbonate indicates a concurrent alkalosis. When the numbers don’t align, there is always another process.

cases.title: Renal failure (uraemic HAGMA with appropriate respiratory compensation)
cases.clinical_stem: 61M from ED with known advanced kidney disease, brought in for reduced urine output over the last day. He looks drowsy but rousable, with no clear chest or abdominal complaint.

cases.learning_objective: Recognise high anion gap metabolic acidosis from uraemia with appropriate respiratory compensation

explanation_blueprint.title: pH Status
explanation_blueprint.body: The pH is < 7.35, consistent with an acidaemia.

explanation_blueprint.title: Primary Disorder
explanation_blueprint.body: HCO3 is 12 mmol/L, which is low and supports a primary metabolic acidosis.

explanation_blueprint.title: Diagnosis
explanation_blueprint.body: This pattern is most consistent with uraemic high anion gap metabolic acidosis from renal failure.

explanation_blueprint.title: Compensation
explanation_blueprint.body: For winter, expected PaCO2 is about 26.0 mmHg (acceptable range 24.0-28.0), and the measured value is 27.8 mmHg, so the compensation is appropriate.

explanation_blueprint.title: Anion Gap Analysis
explanation_blueprint.body: The Anion gap is 136 - (100 + 12) = 24.0, which is raised. A raised anion gap suggests an additional unmeasured acid load. Consider causes such as lactate, ketones, or toxins.

explanation_blueprint.title: Clinical Significance
explanation_blueprint.body: Uraemia causes a raised anion gap metabolic acidosis due to accumulation of unmeasured acids from impaired renal excretion. The anion gap rises as these acids accumulate, and bicarbonate falls accordingly. Respiratory compensation lowers PaCO₂, but the degree of acidosis reflects the severity of renal dysfunction.

explanation_blueprint.title: Key Takeaway
explanation_blueprint.body: Renal failure can explain a high anion gap, but compensation still needs to be checked before calling the picture isolated uraemia.

explanation_blueprint.body: The Anion gap is 139 - (103 + 16) = 20.0, which is raised. A raised anion gap suggests an additional unmeasured acid load. Consider causes such as lactate, ketones, or toxins.

explanation_blueprint.title: Clinical Significance
explanation_blueprint.body: Uraemia causes a raised anion gap metabolic acidosis due to accumulation of unmeasured acids from impaired renal excretion. The anion gap rises as these acids accumulate, and bicarbonate falls accordingly. Respiratory compensation lowers PaCO₂, but the degree of acidosis reflects the severity of renal dysfunction.

explanation_blueprint.title: Key Takeaway
explanation_blueprint.body: Renal failure can explain a high anion gap, but compensation still needs to be checked before calling the picture isolated uraemia.

cases.title: Diuretic use (metabolic alkalosis with respiratory compensation)
cases.clinical_stem: 81M brought to ED with a few days of dizziness and dry mouth after a recent thiazide increase. He looks a bit dehydrated on exam.

questions_flow.label: Primary acid-base disorder
questions_flow.prompt: What is the primary acid-base disorder?

cases.learning_objective: Recognise chloride-responsive metabolic alkalosis from diuretic-associated volume depletion

explanation_blueprint.title: pH Status
explanation_blueprint.body: The pH is in the normal range (7.35 - 7.45).

explanation_blueprint.title: Primary Disorder
explanation_blueprint.body: HCO3 is 30 mmol/L, which is elevated and supports a primary metabolic alkalosis.

explanation_blueprint.title: Diagnosis
explanation_blueprint.body: This pattern fits chloride-responsive metabolic alkalosis. Diuretic use causes volume depletion and chloride loss, which maintains the alkalosis.

explanation_blueprint.title: Compensation
explanation_blueprint.body: For metabolic alkalosis compensation, expected PaCO2 is about 44.2 mmHg (acceptable range 41.2-47.2), and the measured value is 44.3 mmHg, so the compensation is appropriate.

explanation_blueprint.title: Clinical Significance
explanation_blueprint.body: Diuretic use can cause metabolic alkalosis through volume depletion and renal loss of chloride, leading to increased bicarbonate reabsorption. This results in a chloride-responsive metabolic alkalosis, often with low or low-normal potassium. The alkalosis is maintained by ongoing volume and chloride depletion, even after the initial trigger. Recognising this pattern is important, as correction of volume status and chloride deficit is key to reversing the disturbance.

explanation_blueprint.title: pH Status
explanation_blueprint.body: The pH is 7.35, which is near normal - do not let a near-normal pH hide an underlying process.

cases.title: DKA (HAGMA with appropriate respiratory compensation)
cases.clinical_stem: 27-year-old man with type 1 diabetes, brought to ED after missed insulin and a day of polyuria. He’s tachypnoeic with deep breathing on arrival.

questions_flow.label: Primary acid-base disorder
questions_flow.prompt: What is the primary acid-base disorder?

questions_flow.label: Anion gap
questions_flow.prompt: What is the anion gap status?

cases.learning_objective: Recognise high anion gap metabolic acidosis with appropriate respiratory compensation

explanation_blueprint.title: pH Status
explanation_blueprint.body: The pH is < 7.35, consistent with an acidaemia.

explanation_blueprint.title: Primary Disorder
explanation_blueprint.body: HCO3 is 8 mmol/L, which is low and supports a primary metabolic acidosis.

explanation_blueprint.title: Diagnosis
explanation_blueprint.body: The glucose is 33.6 mmol/L, which strongly supports DKA as the cause of this high anion gap metabolic acidosis.

explanation_blueprint.title: Compensation
explanation_blueprint.body: For winter, expected PaCO2 is about 20.0 mmHg (acceptable range 18.0-22.0), and the measured value is 20.4 mmHg, so the compensation is appropriate.

explanation_blueprint.title: Anion Gap Analysis
explanation_blueprint.body: The Anion gap is 133 - (96 + 8) = 29.0, which is raised. A raised anion gap suggests an additional unmeasured acid load. Consider causes such as lactate, ketones, or toxins.

explanation_blueprint.title: Clinical Significance
explanation_blueprint.body: Insulin deficiency leads to unchecked lipolysis and ketone production, resulting in a raised anion gap metabolic acidosis. Marked hyperglycaemia is a key feature, distinguishing DKA from other causes of ketoacidosis. The acidosis is often more severe than in starvation or alcoholic ketoacidosis, and may be accompanied by dehydration and electrolyte disturbances.

explanation_blueprint.title: Key Takeaway
explanation_blueprint.body: High anion gap metabolic acidosis with marked hyperglycaemia suggests DKA, but always consider an additional metabolic process if compensation is inappropriate or the bicarbonate is not as expected.

cases.learning_objective: Recognise respiratory alkalosis with a concurrent high anion gap metabolic acidosis when bicarbonate is too low for isolated respiratory alkalosis

explanation_blueprint.title: pH Status
explanation_blueprint.body: The pH is < 7.35, consistent with an acidaemia.

explanation_blueprint.title: Primary Disorder
explanation_blueprint.body: PaCO2 is 26.4 mmHg, which is low and supports a primary respiratory alkalosis.

explanation_blueprint.title: Diagnosis
explanation_blueprint.body: The overall pattern is respiratory alkalosis with concurrent high anion gap metabolic acidosis.

explanation_blueprint.title: Compensation
explanation_blueprint.body: If this were isolated acute respiratory alkalosis, expected HCO3 would be about 21.3 mmol/L (acceptable range 19.3-23.3), but the measured value is 13.8 mmol/L. That mismatch does not fit a single-process compensation pattern.

explanation_blueprint.title: Anion Gap Analysis
explanation_blueprint.body: The Anion gap is 141 - (99 + 13.8) = 28.2, which is raised. A raised anion gap suggests an additional unmeasured acid load. Consider causes such as lactate, ketones, or toxins.

explanation_blueprint.title: Additional Metabolic Process
explanation_blueprint.body: There is an additional metabolic process present: High anion gap metabolic acidosis. This suggests a mixed disorder beyond simple compensation.

explanation_blueprint.title: Clinical Significance
explanation_blueprint.body: This pattern reflects significant underlying illness with two concurrent processes. A normal or near-normal pH should not reassure, as a serious metabolic acidosis may be masked by hyperventilation. This commonly occurs in conditions such as sepsis, where early respiratory changes precede clinical deterioration.

explanation_blueprint.title: Key Takeaway
explanation_blueprint.body: If a low PaCO2 does not fully explain the acid-base picture, use the anion gap to uncover an added metabolic acidosis.

cases.title: Vomiting (metabolic alkalosis with respiratory compensation)
cases.clinical_stem: ED review of a 24-year-old man with persistent vomiting since overnight, now dry on exam with a bit of epigastric discomfort. He’s alert but looks tired, and an ABG was sent.

questions_flow.label: Primary acid-base disorder
questions_flow.prompt: What is the primary acid-base disorder?

cases.learning_objective: Recognise metabolic alkalosis with appropriate respiratory compensation

explanation_blueprint.title: pH Status
explanation_blueprint.body: The pH is > 7.45, consistent with an alkalaemia.

explanation_blueprint.title: Primary Disorder
explanation_blueprint.body: HCO3 is 33 mmol/L, which is elevated and supports a primary metabolic alkalosis.

explanation_blueprint.title: Diagnosis
explanation_blueprint.body: This pattern fits chloride-responsive metabolic alkalosis from gastric losses.

explanation_blueprint.title: Compensation
explanation_blueprint.body: For metabolic alkalosis compensation, expected PaCO2 is about 46.3 mmHg (acceptable range 43.3-49.3), and the measured value is 44.7 mmHg, so the compensation is appropriate.

explanation_blueprint.title: Clinical Significance
explanation_blueprint.body: Vomiting causes loss of gastric acid, leading to a metabolic alkalosis with elevated bicarbonate and increased pH. The body compensates by hypoventilating to retain CO₂, raising PaCO₂. Ongoing chloride and volume depletion help maintain the alkalosis, so recognising this pattern is important as correction requires replacement of both volume and chloride.

cases.title: Simple respiratory acidosis

explanation_blueprint.title: Clinical Significance
explanation_blueprint.body: Respiratory acidosis is caused by hypoventilation, leading to retention of CO₂ and a fall in pH. Recognising this pattern helps identify inadequate ventilation as the primary problem.

cases.title: Alcoholic ketoacidosis (HAGMA with appropriate respiratory compensation)
cases.clinical_stem: 31M in ED after a recent alcohol binge, with reduced oral intake over the past 3 days. He looks dry and has been tachypnoeic, with an ABG taken on arrival.

questions_flow.label: Anion gap

cases.learning_objective: Recognise alcoholic ketoacidosis as a high anion gap metabolic acidosis with appropriate respiratory compensation

explanation_blueprint.title: pH Status
explanation_blueprint.body: The pH is < 7.35, consistent with an acidaemia.

explanation_blueprint.title: Primary Disorder
explanation_blueprint.body: HCO3 is 18 mmol/L, which is low and supports a primary metabolic acidosis.

explanation_blueprint.title: Diagnosis
explanation_blueprint.body: The history pattern and the high anion gap metabolic acidosis fit alcoholic ketoacidosis.

explanation_blueprint.title: Compensation
explanation_blueprint.body: For winter, expected PaCO2 is about 35.0 mmHg (acceptable range 33.0-37.0), and the measured value is 35.3 mmHg, so the compensation is appropriate.

explanation_blueprint.title: Anion Gap Analysis
explanation_blueprint.body: The Anion gap is 136 - (97 + 18) = 21.0, which is raised. A raised anion gap suggests an additional unmeasured acid load. Consider causes such as lactate, ketones, or toxins.

explanation_blueprint.title: Clinical Significance
explanation_blueprint.body: Increased ketone production in the setting of heavy alcohol use and poor oral intake can cause a raised anion gap metabolic acidosis. Glucose is typically normal or low due to impaired gluconeogenesis, helping distinguish this from DKA. Vomiting may contribute a concurrent metabolic alkalosis, masking the degree of acidaemia. The key clue is recent alcohol use with poor intake, and management focuses on reversing the metabolic state and addressing the underlying cause.

explanation_blueprint.title: Key Takeaway
explanation_blueprint.body: A high anion gap acidosis without marked hyperglycaemia should keep alcoholic ketoacidosis high on the differential list.

cases.learning_objective: Recognise respiratory acidosis with a concurrent high anion gap metabolic acidosis when bicarbonate is too low for isolated respiratory compensation

explanation_blueprint.title: pH Status
explanation_blueprint.body: The pH is < 7.35, consistent with an acidaemia.

explanation_blueprint.title: Primary Disorder
explanation_blueprint.body: PaCO2 is 73.3 mmHg, which is elevated and supports a primary respiratory acidosis.

explanation_blueprint.title: Diagnosis
explanation_blueprint.body: The overall pattern is respiratory acidosis with concurrent high anion gap metabolic acidosis.

explanation_blueprint.title: Compensation
explanation_blueprint.body: If this were isolated chronic respiratory acidosis, expected HCO3 would be about 37.3 mmol/L (acceptable range 35.3-39.3), but the measured value is 26.7 mmol/L. That mismatch does not fit a single-process compensation pattern.

explanation_blueprint.title: Anion Gap Analysis
explanation_blueprint.body: The Anion gap is 140 - (91 + 26.7) = 22.3, which is raised. A raised anion gap suggests an additional unmeasured acid load. Consider causes such as lactate, ketones, or toxins.

explanation_blueprint.title: Additional Metabolic Process
explanation_blueprint.body: There is an additional metabolic process present: High anion gap metabolic acidosis. This suggests a mixed disorder beyond simple compensation.

explanation_blueprint.title: Clinical Significance
explanation_blueprint.body: This pattern suggests severe physiological compromise, with both impaired ventilation and a concurrent metabolic acidosis. It is often seen in critically unwell patients and indicates a high risk of deterioration. Prompt assessment and escalation of care are essential.

explanation_blueprint.title: Key Takeaway
explanation_blueprint.body: When respiratory acidosis and a raised anion gap coexist, expected compensation helps confirm that two primary processes are present.

cases.title: Toxic alcohol (HAGMA with appropriate respiratory compensation)
cases.clinical_stem: 41F in ED with confusion and blurred vision after a possible ingestion; a solvent container was found nearby. She’s tachypnoeic but otherwise not giving much history.

cases.learning_objective: Recognise toxic alcohol ingestion as a high anion gap metabolic acidosis with appropriate respiratory compensation

explanation_blueprint.title: pH Status
explanation_blueprint.body: The pH is < 7.35, consistent with an acidaemia.

explanation_blueprint.title: Primary Disorder
explanation_blueprint.body: HCO3 is 13 mmol/L, which is low and supports a primary metabolic acidosis.

explanation_blueprint.title: Diagnosis
explanation_blueprint.body: In the appropriate clinical context, a high anion gap metabolic acidosis should prompt consideration of toxic alcohol ingestion.

explanation_blueprint.title: Compensation
explanation_blueprint.body: For winter, expected PaCO2 is about 27.5 mmHg (acceptable range 25.5-29.5), and the measured value is 27.4 mmHg, so the compensation is appropriate.

explanation_blueprint.title: Anion Gap Analysis
explanation_blueprint.body: The Anion gap is 138 - (99 + 13) = 26.0, which is raised. A raised anion gap suggests an additional unmeasured acid load. Consider causes such as lactate, ketones, or toxins.

explanation_blueprint.title: Clinical Significance
explanation_blueprint.body: Toxic alcohol ingestion can cause a severe raised anion gap metabolic acidosis due to accumulation of acidic metabolites. Common agents include methanol and ethylene glycol. The degree of acidosis is often disproportionate to any measured lactate, and may worsen rapidly as metabolism progresses. The key clinical clue is an unexplained high anion gap acidosis without a clear metabolic cause. This is a time-critical diagnosis, and early recognition is essential to prevent ongoing toxicity and organ injury.

explanation_blueprint.title: Key Takeaway
explanation_blueprint.body: An unexplained severe high anion gap metabolic acidosis — especially if disproportionate to lactate, should prompt urgent consideration of toxic alcohol ingestion.

explanation_blueprint.title: Clinical Significance
explanation_blueprint.body: Diarrhoea causes a normal anion gap metabolic acidosis through direct loss of bicarbonate from the GI tract. explanation_blueprint.body: The acidosis fits a normal anion gap pattern, but think bicarbonate loss from the gut rather than unmeasured acids.

explanation_blueprint.title: Clinical Significance
explanation_blueprint.body: Vomiting causes loss of gastric acid, leading to a metabolic alkalosis with elevated bicarbonate and increased pH. answer_key.final_diagnosis: Respiratory alkalosis with concurrent high anion gap metabolic acidosis

cases.learning_objective: Recognise respiratory alkalosis with a concurrent high anion gap metabolic acidosis when bicarbonate is too low for isolated respiratory alkalosis

explanation_blueprint.title: pH Status
explanation_blueprint.body: The pH is 7.41, which is near normal - do not let a near-normal pH hide an underlying process.

explanation_blueprint.title: Primary Disorder
explanation_blueprint.body: PaCO2 is 29.0 mmHg, which is low and supports a primary respiratory alkalosis.

explanation_blueprint.title: Diagnosis
explanation_blueprint.body: The overall pattern is respiratory alkalosis with concurrent high anion gap metabolic acidosis.

explanation_blueprint.title: Compensation
explanation_blueprint.body: If this were isolated acute respiratory alkalosis, expected HCO3 would be about 21.8 mmol/L (acceptable range 19.8-23.8), but the measured value is 17.9 mmol/L. That mismatch does not fit a single-process compensation pattern.

explanation_blueprint.title: Anion Gap Analysis
explanation_blueprint.body: The Anion gap is 139 - (101 + 17.9) = 20.1, which is raised. A raised anion gap suggests an additional unmeasured acid load. Consider causes such as lactate, ketones, or toxins.

explanation_blueprint.title: Additional Metabolic Process
explanation_blueprint.body: There is an additional metabolic process present: High anion gap metabolic acidosis. This suggests a mixed disorder beyond simple compensation.

explanation_blueprint.title: Clinical Significance
explanation_blueprint.body: This pattern reflects significant underlying illness with two concurrent processes. A normal or near-normal pH should not reassure, as a serious metabolic acidosis may be masked by hyperventilation. This commonly occurs in conditions such as sepsis, where early respiratory changes precede clinical deterioration.

explanation_blueprint.title: Key Takeaway
explanation_blueprint.body: If a low PaCO2 does not fully explain the acid-base picture, use the anion gap to uncover an added metabolic acidosis.

cases.title: Starvation ketosis (subtle HAGMA with appropriate respiratory compensation)
cases.clinical_stem: 43-year-old man in ED with a few days of poor oral intake and weight loss, now complaining of fatigue and weakness. He looks dehydrated on exam, and an ABG was sent.

questions_flow.label: Primary acid-base disorder
questions_flow.prompt: What is the primary acid-base disorder?

questions_flow.label: Anion gap
questions_flow.prompt: What is the anion gap status?

cases.learning_objective: Recognise starvation ketosis as a subtle high anion gap metabolic acidosis with appropriate respiratory compensation and normal-range glucose

explanation_blueprint.title: pH Status
explanation_blueprint.body: The pH is < 7.35, consistent with an acidaemia.

explanation_blueprint.title: Primary Disorder
explanation_blueprint.body: HCO3 is 17 mmol/L, which is low and supports a primary metabolic acidosis.

explanation_blueprint.title: Diagnosis
explanation_blueprint.body: The glucose is 4.0 mmol/L, which is not markedly elevated. This is more in keeping with starvation ketosis over DKA.

explanation_blueprint.title: Compensation
explanation_blueprint.body: For winter, expected PaCO2 is about 33.5 mmHg (acceptable range 31.5-35.5), and the measured value is 34.1 mmHg, so the compensation is appropriate.

explanation_blueprint.title: Anion Gap Analysis
explanation_blueprint.body: The Anion gap is 136 - (101 + 17) = 18.0, which is raised. A raised anion gap suggests an additional unmeasured acid load. Consider causes such as lactate, ketones, or toxins.

explanation_blueprint.title: Clinical Significance
explanation_blueprint.body: Increased ketone production in the setting of poor oral intake can cause a raised anion gap metabolic acidosis. This is typically milder than DKA because some endogenous insulin activity remains, limiting unchecked ketogenesis. The key clinical clue is a history of reduced intake with normal or only mildly elevated glucose. This pattern should prompt correction of the fasting state and assessment for the underlying reason the patient has not been eating.

explanation_blueprint.title: Key Takeaway
explanation_blueprint.body: When ketosis occurs without major hyperglycaemia, use the clinical context to separate starvation ketosis from DKA.

cases.title: Diuretic use (metabolic alkalosis with respiratory compensation)
cases.clinical_stem: 72M in ED with dizziness and dry mucous membranes after a recent increase in his thiazide dose. He looks mildly dehydrated on exam.

questions_flow.label: Primary acid-base disorder
questions_flow.prompt: What is the primary acid-base disorder?

cases.learning_objective: Recognise chloride-responsive metabolic alkalosis from diuretic-associated volume depletion

explanation_blueprint.title: pH Status
explanation_blueprint.body: The pH is > 7.45, consistent with an alkalaemia.

explanation_blueprint.title: Primary Disorder
explanation_blueprint.body: HCO3 is 36 mmol/L, which is elevated and supports a primary metabolic alkalosis.

explanation_blueprint.title: Diagnosis
explanation_blueprint.body: This pattern fits chloride-responsive metabolic alkalosis. Diuretic use causes volume depletion and chloride loss, which maintains the alkalosis.

explanation_blueprint.title: Compensation
explanation_blueprint.body: For metabolic alkalosis compensation, expected PaCO2 is about 48.4 mmHg (acceptable range 45.4-51.4), and the measured value is 50.5 mmHg, so the compensation is appropriate.

explanation_blueprint.title: Clinical Significance
explanation_blueprint.body: Diuretic use can cause metabolic alkalosis through volume depletion and renal loss of chloride, leading to increased bicarbonate reabsorption. This results in a chloride-responsive metabolic alkalosis, often with low or low-normal potassium. The alkalosis is maintained by ongoing volume and chloride depletion, even after the initial trigger. Recognising this pattern is important, as correction of volume status and chloride deficit is key to reversing the disturbance.

cases.learning_objective: Recognise a simple bicarbonate-driven metabolic acidosis with a normal anion gap

explanation_blueprint.title: pH Status
explanation_blueprint.body: The pH is < 7.35, consistent with an acidaemia.

explanation_blueprint.title: Primary Disorder
explanation_blueprint.body: HCO3 is 14 mmol/L, which is low and supports a primary metabolic acidosis.

explanation_blueprint.title: Diagnosis
explanation_blueprint.body: This pattern fits gastrointestinal bicarbonate loss causing a normal anion gap metabolic acidosis.

explanation_blueprint.title: Clinical Significance
explanation_blueprint.body: A normal anion gap metabolic acidosis reflects loss of bicarbonate or impaired renal acid excretion rather than accumulation of unmeasured acids. The chloride rises to maintain electroneutrality, resulting in a hyperchloraemic acidosis. Common causes include gastrointestinal bicarbonate loss or renal tubular dysfunction.

cases.learning_objective: Recognise normal anion gap metabolic acidosis with appropriate respiratory compensation

explanation_blueprint.title: pH Status
explanation_blueprint.body: The pH is < 7.35, consistent with an acidaemia.

explanation_blueprint.title: Primary Disorder
explanation_blueprint.body: HCO3 is 12 mmol/L, which is low and supports a primary metabolic acidosis.

explanation_blueprint.title: Diagnosis
explanation_blueprint.body: This pattern fits gastrointestinal bicarbonate loss causing a normal anion gap metabolic acidosis.

explanation_blueprint.title: Compensation
explanation_blueprint.body: For winter, expected PaCO2 is about 26.0 mmHg (acceptable range 24.0-28.0), and the measured value is 27.9 mmHg, so the compensation is appropriate.

explanation_blueprint.title: Anion Gap Analysis
explanation_blueprint.body: The Anion gap is 137 - (113 + 12) = 12.0, which is normal.

explanation_blueprint.title: Clinical Significance
explanation_blueprint.body: Diarrhoea causes a normal anion gap metabolic acidosis through direct loss of bicarbonate from the GI tract. This results in a hyperchloraemic acidosis as chloride rises to maintain electroneutrality. The pattern is typically accompanied by volume depletion and may be associated with low potassium. Recognising this mechanism helps distinguish it from renal causes of bicarbonate loss and from high anion gap acidoses.

explanation_blueprint.title: Key Takeaway
explanation_blueprint.body: The acidosis fits a normal anion gap pattern, but think bicarbonate loss from the gut rather than unmeasured acids.

cases.learning_objective: Recognise acute respiratory acidosis due to hypoventilation with appropriate compensation

explanation_blueprint.title: pH Status
explanation_blueprint.body: The pH is < 7.35, consistent with an acidaemia.

explanation_blueprint.title: Primary Disorder
explanation_blueprint.body: PaCO2 is 75 mmHg, which is elevated and supports a primary respiratory acidosis.

explanation_blueprint.title: Diagnosis
explanation_blueprint.body: This pattern fits opioid-related hypoventilation.

explanation_blueprint.title: Clinical Significance
explanation_blueprint.body: Opioid toxicity causes respiratory depression, leading to reduced ventilation and retention of CO₂. This results in a respiratory acidosis with an elevated PaCO₂ and a fall in pH. The key problem is inadequate ventilation, and early recognition is important as this is a reversible cause of respiratory failure.

cases.title: Renal failure (uraemic HAGMA with appropriate respiratory compensation)
cases.clinical_stem: ED handover on a 68-year-old man with advanced kidney disease and reduced urine output over the past day. He’s been increasingly weak and nauseated, with some drowsiness noted by family.

cases.learning_objective: Recognise high anion gap metabolic acidosis from uraemia with appropriate respiratory compensation

explanation_blueprint.title: pH Status
explanation_blueprint.body: The pH is < 7.35, consistent with an acidaemia.

explanation_blueprint.title: Primary Disorder
explanation_blueprint.body: HCO3 is 11 mmol/L, which is low and supports a primary metabolic acidosis.

explanation_blueprint.title: Diagnosis
explanation_blueprint.body: This pattern is most consistent with uraemic high anion gap metabolic acidosis from renal failure.

explanation_blueprint.title: Compensation
explanation_blueprint.body: For winter, expected PaCO2 is about 24.5 mmHg (acceptable range 22.5-26.5), and the measured value is 24.2 mmHg, so the compensation is appropriate.

explanation_blueprint.title: Anion Gap Analysis
explanation_blueprint.body: The Anion gap is 137 - (96 + 11) = 30.0, which is raised. A raised anion gap suggests an additional unmeasured acid load. Consider causes such as lactate, ketones, or toxins.

explanation_blueprint.title: Clinical Significance
explanation_blueprint.body: Uraemia causes a raised anion gap metabolic acidosis due to accumulation of unmeasured acids from impaired renal excretion. The anion gap rises as these acids accumulate, and bicarbonate falls accordingly. Respiratory compensation lowers PaCO₂, but the degree of acidosis reflects the severity of renal dysfunction.

explanation_blueprint.title: Key Takeaway
explanation_blueprint.body: Renal failure can explain a high anion gap, but compensation still needs to be checked before calling the picture isolated uraemia.

The acidosis fits a normal anion gap pattern, but think bicarbonate loss from the gut rather than unmeasured acids.

cases.learning_objective: Recognise the classic respiratory alkalosis plus high anion gap metabolic acidosis pattern of salicylate toxicity

explanation_blueprint.title: pH Status
explanation_blueprint.body: The pH is < 7.35, consistent with an acidaemia.

explanation_blueprint.title: Primary Disorder
explanation_blueprint.body: PaCO2 is 27.2 mmHg, which is low and supports a primary respiratory alkalosis.

explanation_blueprint.title: Diagnosis
explanation_blueprint.body: This is the classic salicylate pattern: respiratory alkalosis with concurrent high anion gap metabolic acidosis.

explanation_blueprint.title: Compensation
explanation_blueprint.body: If this were isolated acute respiratory alkalosis, expected HCO3 would be about 21.4 mmol/L (acceptable range 19.4-23.4), but the measured value is 13.9 mmol/L. That mismatch does not fit a single-process compensation pattern.

explanation_blueprint.title: Anion Gap Analysis
explanation_blueprint.body: The Anion gap is 138 - (94 + 13.9) = 30.1, which is raised. A raised anion gap suggests an additional unmeasured acid load. Consider causes such as lactate, ketones, or toxins.

explanation_blueprint.title: Additional Metabolic Process
explanation_blueprint.body: There is an additional metabolic process present: High anion gap metabolic acidosis. This suggests a mixed disorder beyond simple compensation.

explanation_blueprint.title: Clinical Significance
explanation_blueprint.body: This pattern is characteristic of salicylate toxicity, where early hyperventilation is followed by metabolic acidosis. The combination should prompt urgent consideration of overdose, even if the history is unclear. Early recognition is critical, as clinical deterioration can be rapid.

explanation_blueprint.title: Key Takeaway
explanation_blueprint.body: Salicylate cases are high-risk because the alkalosis and acidosis co-exist, so compensation formulas help expose the mixed picture quickly.

cases.title: Alcoholic ketoacidosis (HAGMA with appropriate respiratory compensation)
cases.clinical_stem: 48M brought to ED after a recent alcohol binge with a couple of days of poor oral intake and repeated vomiting. He’s tachypnoeic and looks unwell, but no clear diabetic history.

questions_flow.label: Primary acid-base disorder

cases.learning_objective: Recognise alcoholic ketoacidosis as a high anion gap metabolic acidosis with appropriate respiratory compensation

explanation_blueprint.title: pH Status
explanation_blueprint.body: The pH is < 7.35, consistent with an acidaemia.

explanation_blueprint.title: Primary Disorder
explanation_blueprint.body: HCO3 is 8 mmol/L, which is low and supports a primary metabolic acidosis.

explanation_blueprint.title: Diagnosis
explanation_blueprint.body: The history pattern and the high anion gap metabolic acidosis fit alcoholic ketoacidosis.

explanation_blueprint.title: Compensation
explanation_blueprint.body: For winter, expected PaCO2 is about 20.0 mmHg (acceptable range 18.0-22.0), and the measured value is 19.8 mmHg, so the compensation is appropriate.

explanation_blueprint.title: Anion Gap Analysis
explanation_blueprint.body: The Anion gap is 128 - (88 + 8) = 32.0, which is raised. A raised anion gap suggests an additional unmeasured acid load. Consider causes such as lactate, ketones, or toxins.

explanation_blueprint.title: Clinical Significance
explanation_blueprint.body: Increased ketone production in the setting of heavy alcohol use and poor oral intake can cause a raised anion gap metabolic acidosis. Glucose is typically normal or low due to impaired gluconeogenesis, helping distinguish this from DKA. Vomiting may contribute a concurrent metabolic alkalosis, masking the degree of acidaemia. The key clue is recent alcohol use with poor intake, and management focuses on reversing the metabolic state and addressing the underlying cause.

explanation_blueprint.title: Key Takeaway
explanation_blueprint.body: A high anion gap acidosis without marked hyperglycaemia should keep alcoholic ketoacidosis high on the differential list.

explanation_blueprint.body: The pH is < 7.35, consistent with an acidaemia.

explanation_blueprint.body: PaCO2 is 65.0 mmHg, which is elevated and supports a primary respiratory acidosis.

explanation_blueprint.title: Compensation
explanation_blueprint.body: If this were isolated chronic respiratory acidosis, expected HCO3 would be about 34.0 mmol/L (acceptable range 32.0-36.0), but the measured value is 22.9 mmol/L. That mismatch does not fit a single-process compensation pattern.

explanation_blueprint.title: Anion Gap Analysis
explanation_blueprint.body: The Anion gap is 141 - (95 + 22.9) = 23.1, which is raised. A raised anion gap suggests an additional unmeasured acid load. Consider causes such as lactate, ketones, or toxins.

explanation_blueprint.title: Key Takeaway
explanation_blueprint.body: When respiratory acidosis and a raised anion gap coexist, expected compensation helps confirm that two primary processes are present.

cases.title: Toxic alcohol (HAGMA with appropriate respiratory compensation)
cases.clinical_stem: 55F brought to ED with confusion and blurred vision after a possible ingestion at home; family found a suspicious solvent container nearby. She also reports upper abdominal pain.

questions_flow.label: Primary acid-base disorder

cases.learning_objective: Recognise toxic alcohol ingestion as a high anion gap metabolic acidosis with appropriate respiratory compensation

explanation_blueprint.title: pH Status
explanation_blueprint.body: The pH is < 7.35, consistent with an acidaemia.

explanation_blueprint.title: Primary Disorder
explanation_blueprint.body: HCO3 is 16 mmol/L, which is low and supports a primary metabolic acidosis.

explanation_blueprint.title: Diagnosis
explanation_blueprint.body: In the appropriate clinical context, a high anion gap metabolic acidosis should prompt consideration of toxic alcohol ingestion.

explanation_blueprint.title: Compensation
explanation_blueprint.body: For winter, expected PaCO2 is about 32.0 mmHg (acceptable range 30.0-34.0), and the measured value is 33.8 mmHg, so the compensation is appropriate.

explanation_blueprint.title: Anion Gap Analysis
explanation_blueprint.body: The Anion gap is 144 - (104 + 16) = 24.0, which is raised. A raised anion gap suggests an additional unmeasured acid load. Consider causes such as lactate, ketones, or toxins.

explanation_blueprint.title: Clinical Significance
explanation_blueprint.body: Toxic alcohol ingestion can cause a severe raised anion gap metabolic acidosis due to accumulation of acidic metabolites. Common agents include methanol and ethylene glycol. The degree of acidosis is often disproportionate to any measured lactate, and may worsen rapidly as metabolism progresses. The key clinical clue is an unexplained high anion gap acidosis without a clear metabolic cause. This is a time-critical diagnosis, and early recognition is essential to prevent ongoing toxicity and organ injury.

explanation_blueprint.title: Key Takeaway
explanation_blueprint.body: An unexplained severe high anion gap metabolic acidosis — especially if disproportionate to lactate, should prompt urgent consideration of toxic alcohol ingestion.

cases.title: Vomiting (metabolic alkalosis with respiratory compensation)
cases.clinical_stem: 35M in ED with several days of persistent vomiting and retching, now tired and mildly dehydrated on arrival. No diarrhoea or fever reported.

questions_flow.label: Primary acid-base disorder
questions_flow.prompt: What is the primary acid-base disorder?

cases.learning_objective: Recognise metabolic alkalosis with appropriate respiratory compensation

explanation_blueprint.title: Primary Disorder
explanation_blueprint.body: HCO3 is 37 mmol/L, which is elevated and supports a primary metabolic alkalosis.

explanation_blueprint.title: Clinical Significance
explanation_blueprint.body: Vomiting causes loss of gastric acid, leading to a metabolic alkalosis with elevated bicarbonate and increased pH. Ongoing chloride and volume depletion help maintain the alkalosis, so recognising this pattern is important as correction requires replacement of both volume and chloride.

explanation_blueprint.body: This pattern fits simple acute hypoventilation.

explanation_blueprint.title: Clinical Significance
explanation_blueprint.body: Respiratory acidosis is caused by hypoventilation, leading to retention of CO₂ and a fall in pH. Recognising this pattern helps identify inadequate ventilation as the primary problem.

cases.title: DKA with vomiting (mixed metabolic disorder)
cases.clinical_stem: 24-year-old man with type 1 diabetes came into ED after a few episodes of vomiting. He’s missed insulin for the last day and is breathing a bit deep and fast on arrival.

questions_flow.label: Primary acid-base disorder

explanation_blueprint.title: pH Status
explanation_blueprint.body: The pH is < 7.35, consistent with an acidaemia.

explanation_blueprint.title: Primary Disorder
explanation_blueprint.body: HCO3 is 12 mmol/L, which is low and supports a primary metabolic acidosis.

explanation_blueprint.title: Diagnosis
explanation_blueprint.body: This fits DKA with a concurrent metabolic alkalosis from vomiting rather than an isolated ketoacidosis.

explanation_blueprint.title: Compensation
explanation_blueprint.body: For winter, expected PaCO2 is about 26.0 mmHg (acceptable range 24.0-28.0), and the measured value is 26.0 mmHg, so the compensation is appropriate.

explanation_blueprint.title: Anion Gap Analysis
explanation_blueprint.body: The Anion gap is 137 - (95 + 12) = 30.0, which is raised. A raised anion gap suggests an additional unmeasured acid load. Consider causes such as lactate, ketones, or toxins.

explanation_blueprint.title: Additional Metabolic Process
explanation_blueprint.body: There is an additional metabolic process present: Metabolic alkalosis. This suggests a mixed disorder beyond simple compensation.

explanation_blueprint.title: Clinical Significance
explanation_blueprint.body: Insulin deficiency drives ketone production, causing a raised anion gap metabolic acidosis consistent with DKA. However, concurrent vomiting leads to loss of gastric acid and a superimposed metabolic alkalosis, which can partially offset the acidaemia and result in a higher-than-expected bicarbonate. This mixed picture may make the acidosis appear less severe than expected for the degree of hyperglycaemia.

explanation_blueprint.title: Key Takeaway
explanation_blueprint.body: A preserved bicarbonate in DKA should prompt a search for a concurrent alkalosis rather than reassurance that the acidosis is mild.

cases.title: Alcoholic ketoacidosis (HAGMA with appropriate respiratory compensation)
cases.clinical_stem: 26F in ED after a recent alcohol binge with reduced oral intake for 3 days. She’s been vomiting and looks dry on exam, with mild tachypnoea.
