cases.learning_objective: Recognise alcoholic ketoacidosis as a high anion gap metabolic acidosis with appropriate respiratory compensation

explanation_blueprint.title: pH Status
explanation_blueprint.body: The pH is < 7.35, consistent with an acidaemia.

explanation_blueprint.title: Primary Disorder
explanation_blueprint.body: HCO3 is 12 mmol/L, which is low and supports a primary metabolic acidosis.

explanation_blueprint.title: Diagnosis
explanation_blueprint.body: The history pattern and the high anion gap metabolic acidosis fit alcoholic ketoacidosis.

explanation_blueprint.title: Compensation
explanation_blueprint.body: For winter, expected PaCO2 is about 26.0 mmHg (acceptable range 24.0-28.0), and the measured value is 26.0 mmHg, so the compensation is appropriate.

explanation_blueprint.title: Anion Gap Analysis
explanation_blueprint.body: The Anion gap is 130 - (92 + 12) = 26.0, which is raised. A raised anion gap suggests an additional unmeasured acid load. Consider causes such as lactate, ketones, or toxins.

explanation_blueprint.title: Clinical Significance
explanation_blueprint.body: Increased ketone production in the setting of heavy alcohol use and poor oral intake can cause a raised anion gap metabolic acidosis. Glucose is typically normal or low due to impaired gluconeogenesis, helping distinguish this from DKA. Vomiting may contribute a concurrent metabolic alkalosis, masking the degree of acidaemia. The key clue is recent alcohol use with poor intake, and management focuses on reversing the metabolic state and addressing the underlying cause.

explanation_blueprint.title: Key Takeaway
explanation_blueprint.body: A high anion gap acidosis without marked hyperglycaemia should keep alcoholic ketoacidosis high on the differential list.

cases.title: Lactic acidosis (sepsis)
cases.clinical_stem: 65F brought in by ambulance with hypotension and a cold, mottled peripheries. She’s tachycardic and looks unwell, with an ABG sent in ED.

questions_flow.label: Primary acid-base disorder
questions_flow.prompt: What is the primary acid-base disorder?

questions_flow.label: Anion gap
questions_flow.prompt: What is the anion gap status?

explanation_blueprint.title: pH Status
explanation_blueprint.body: The pH is < 7.35, consistent with an acidaemia.

explanation_blueprint.title: Primary Disorder
explanation_blueprint.body: HCO3 is 11.2 mmol/L, which is low and supports a primary metabolic acidosis.

explanation_blueprint.title: Diagnosis
explanation_blueprint.body: Lactate is 8.5 mmol/L, which supports lactic acidosis in the septic clinical context.

explanation_blueprint.title: Compensation
explanation_blueprint.body: For winter, expected PaCO2 is about 24.8 mmHg (acceptable range 22.8-26.8), and the measured value is 24.3 mmHg, so the compensation is appropriate.

explanation_blueprint.title: Anion Gap Analysis
explanation_blueprint.body: The Anion gap is 142 - (98 + 11.2) = 32.8, which is raised. A raised anion gap suggests an additional unmeasured acid load. Consider causes such as lactate, ketones, or toxins.

explanation_blueprint.title: Clinical Significance
explanation_blueprint.body: Lactic acidosis results from impaired tissue oxygen delivery or utilisation, leading to accumulation of lactate and a raised anion gap metabolic acidosis. Common causes include septic or cardiogenic shock, severe anaemia, or regional ischaemia. The elevated lactate reflects underlying hypoperfusion or cellular dysfunction. This is a marker of significant physiological stress, and urgent identification and treatment of the underlying cause is critical.

explanation_blueprint.title: Key Takeaway
explanation_blueprint.body: A raised lactate explains the gap, but the urgent job is still to find and reverse the cause of hypoperfusion or tissue stress.

cases.title: Diarrhoea (normal anion gap metabolic acidosis)
cases.clinical_stem: 34-year-old man in the ED with several days of diarrhoea and light-headedness. He looks mildly dehydrated, with dry mucous membranes and reduced oral intake.

questions_flow.label: Primary acid-base disorder
questions_flow.prompt: What is the primary acid-base disorder?

questions_flow.label: Anion gap
questions_flow.prompt: What is the anion gap status?

cases.learning_objective: Recognise normal anion gap metabolic acidosis with appropriate respiratory compensation

explanation_blueprint.title: pH Status
explanation_blueprint.body: The pH is < 7.35, consistent with an acidaemia.

explanation_blueprint.title: Primary Disorder
explanation_blueprint.body: HCO3 is 20 mmol/L, which is low and supports a primary metabolic acidosis.

explanation_blueprint.title: Diagnosis
explanation_blueprint.body: This pattern fits gastrointestinal bicarbonate loss causing a normal anion gap metabolic acidosis.

explanation_blueprint.title: Compensation
explanation_blueprint.body: For winter, expected PaCO2 is about 38.0 mmHg (acceptable range 36.0-40.0), and the measured value is 38.8 mmHg, so the compensation is appropriate.

explanation_blueprint.title: Anion Gap Analysis
explanation_blueprint.body: The Anion gap is 142 - (112 + 20) = 10.0, which is normal.

explanation_blueprint.title: Clinical Significance
explanation_blueprint.body: Diarrhoea causes a normal anion gap metabolic acidosis through direct loss of bicarbonate from the GI tract. This results in a hyperchloraemic acidosis as chloride rises to maintain electroneutrality. The pattern is typically accompanied by volume depletion and may be associated with low potassium. Recognising this mechanism helps distinguish it from renal causes of bicarbonate loss and from high anion gap acidoses.

explanation_blueprint.title: Key Takeaway
explanation_blueprint.body: The acidosis fits a normal anion gap pattern, but think bicarbonate loss from the gut rather than unmeasured acids.

cases.title: Opioid toxicity (acute respiratory acidosis)
cases.clinical_stem: A 32-year-old woman was brought into ED after collapsing at home and is difficult to rouse. She has a low GCS, shallow respirations and pinpoint pupils.

explanation_blueprint.body: PaCO2 is 55 mmHg, which is elevated and supports a primary respiratory acidosis.

explanation_blueprint.title: Diagnosis
explanation_blueprint.body: This pattern fits opioid-related hypoventilation.

explanation_blueprint.title: Clinical Significance
explanation_blueprint.body: Opioid toxicity causes respiratory depression, leading to reduced ventilation and retention of CO₂. This results in a respiratory acidosis with an elevated PaCO₂ and a fall in pH. The key problem is inadequate ventilation, and early recognition is important as this is a reversible cause of respiratory failure.

cases.title: Renal failure (uraemic HAGMA with appropriate respiratory compensation)
cases.clinical_stem: 66F in ED with advanced kidney disease and reduced urine output over the last few days. She’s been increasingly weak and nauseated, and is a bit drowsy on arrival.

questions_flow.label: Primary acid-base disorder
questions_flow.prompt: What is the primary acid-base disorder?

questions_flow.label: Anion gap
questions_flow.prompt: What is the anion gap status?

cases.learning_objective: Recognise high anion gap metabolic acidosis from uraemia with appropriate respiratory compensation

explanation_blueprint.title: pH Status
explanation_blueprint.body: The pH is < 7.35, consistent with an acidaemia.

explanation_blueprint.title: Primary Disorder
explanation_blueprint.body: HCO3 is 15 mmol/L, which is low and supports a primary metabolic acidosis.

explanation_blueprint.title: Diagnosis
explanation_blueprint.body: This pattern is most consistent with uraemic high anion gap metabolic acidosis from renal failure.

explanation_blueprint.title: Compensation
explanation_blueprint.body: For winter, expected PaCO2 is about 30.5 mmHg (acceptable range 28.5-32.5), and the measured value is 29.1 mmHg, so the compensation is appropriate.

explanation_blueprint.title: Anion Gap Analysis
explanation_blueprint.body: The Anion gap is 140 - (107 + 15) = 18.0, which is raised. A raised anion gap suggests an additional unmeasured acid load. Consider causes such as lactate, ketones, or toxins.

explanation_blueprint.title: Clinical Significance
explanation_blueprint.body: Uraemia causes a raised anion gap metabolic acidosis due to accumulation of unmeasured acids from impaired renal excretion. The anion gap rises as these acids accumulate, and bicarbonate falls accordingly. Respiratory compensation lowers PaCO₂, but the degree of acidosis reflects the severity of renal dysfunction.

explanation_blueprint.title: Key Takeaway
explanation_blueprint.body: Renal failure can explain a high anion gap, but compensation still needs to be checked before calling the picture isolated uraemia.

cases.title: Diuretic use (metabolic alkalosis with respiratory compensation)
cases.clinical_stem: 81-year-old man in ED with a recent increase in his diuretic dose, now reporting dizziness and fatigue. He looks dry on exam and is awaiting an ABG review.

questions_flow.label: Primary acid-base disorder
questions_flow.prompt: What is the primary acid-base disorder?

cases.learning_objective: Recognise chloride-responsive metabolic alkalosis from diuretic-associated volume depletion

explanation_blueprint.title: pH Status
explanation_blueprint.body: The pH is > 7.45, consistent with an alkalaemia.

explanation_blueprint.title: Primary Disorder
explanation_blueprint.body: HCO3 is 30 mmol/L, which is elevated and supports a primary metabolic alkalosis.

explanation_blueprint.title: Diagnosis
explanation_blueprint.body: This pattern fits chloride-responsive metabolic alkalosis. Diuretic use causes volume depletion and chloride loss, which maintains the alkalosis.

explanation_blueprint.title: Compensation
explanation_blueprint.body: For metabolic alkalosis compensation, expected PaCO2 is about 44.2 mmHg (acceptable range 41.2-47.2), and the measured value is 43.3 mmHg, so the compensation is appropriate.

explanation_blueprint.title: Clinical Significance
explanation_blueprint.body: Diuretic use can cause metabolic alkalosis through volume depletion and renal loss of chloride, leading to increased bicarbonate reabsorption. This results in a chloride-responsive metabolic alkalosis, often with low or low-normal potassium. The alkalosis is maintained by ongoing volume and chloride depletion, even after the initial trigger. Recognising this pattern is important, as correction of volume status and chloride deficit is key to reversing the disturbance.

cases.title: Respiratory alkalosis with concurrent HAGMA
cases.clinical_stem: 44F in ED, unwell with tachypnoea and diffuse abdominal discomfort. RR is high and she looks unsettled, but no obvious wheeze or chest findings on initial review.

options: Respiratory alkalosis with concurrent high anion gap metabolic acidosis

answer_key.final_diagnosis: Respiratory alkalosis with concurrent high anion gap metabolic acidosis

cases.learning_objective: Recognise respiratory alkalosis with a concurrent high anion gap metabolic acidosis when bicarbonate is too low for isolated respiratory alkalosis

explanation_blueprint.title: pH Status
explanation_blueprint.body: The pH is 7.37, which is near normal - do not let a near-normal pH hide an underlying process.

explanation_blueprint.title: Primary Disorder
explanation_blueprint.body: PaCO2 is 23.3 mmHg, which is low and supports a primary respiratory alkalosis.

explanation_blueprint.title: Diagnosis
explanation_blueprint.body: The overall pattern is respiratory alkalosis with concurrent high anion gap metabolic acidosis.

explanation_blueprint.title: Compensation
explanation_blueprint.body: If this were isolated acute respiratory alkalosis, expected HCO3 would be about 20.7 mmol/L (acceptable range 18.7-22.7), but the measured value is 12.9 mmol/L. That mismatch does not fit a single-process compensation pattern.

explanation_blueprint.title: Anion Gap Analysis
explanation_blueprint.body: The Anion gap is 137 - (97 + 12.9) = 27.1, which is raised. A raised anion gap suggests an additional unmeasured acid load. Consider causes such as lactate, ketones, or toxins.

explanation_blueprint.title: Additional Metabolic Process
explanation_blueprint.body: There is an additional metabolic process present: High anion gap metabolic acidosis. This suggests a mixed disorder beyond simple compensation.

explanation_blueprint.title: Clinical Significance
explanation_blueprint.body: This pattern reflects significant underlying illness with two concurrent processes. A normal or near-normal pH should not reassure, as a serious metabolic acidosis may be masked by hyperventilation. This commonly occurs in conditions such as sepsis, where early respiratory changes precede clinical deterioration.

explanation_blueprint.title: Key Takeaway
explanation_blueprint.body: If a low PaCO2 does not fully explain the acid-base picture, use the anion gap to uncover an added metabolic acidosis.

explanation_blueprint.title: Clinical Significance
explanation_blueprint.body: Increased ketone production in the setting of heavy alcohol use and poor oral intake can cause a raised anion gap metabolic acidosis. Glucose is typically normal or low due to impaired gluconeogenesis, helping distinguish this from DKA. Vomiting may contribute a concurrent metabolic alkalosis, masking the degree of acidaemia. The key clue is recent alcohol use with poor intake, and management focuses on reversing the metabolic state and addressing the underlying cause.

explanation_blueprint.title: Key Takeaway
explanation_blueprint.body: A high anion gap acidosis without marked hyperglycaemia should keep alcoholic ketoacidosis high on the differential list.

cases.title: Lactic acidosis (sepsis)
cases.clinical_stem: 60M brought to ED from the ward with hypotension and a rising HR, looking mottled and confused. He’s in shock and the team are concerned about ongoing tissue hypoperfusion.

questions_flow.label: Primary acid-base disorder
questions_flow.prompt: What is the primary acid-base disorder?

questions_flow.label: Anion gap
questions_flow.prompt: What is the anion gap status?

explanation_blueprint.title: pH Status
explanation_blueprint.body: The pH is < 7.35, consistent with an acidaemia.

explanation_blueprint.title: Primary Disorder
explanation_blueprint.body: HCO3 is 14.7 mmol/L, which is low and supports a primary metabolic acidosis.

explanation_blueprint.title: Diagnosis
explanation_blueprint.body: Lactate is 4.2 mmol/L, which supports lactic acidosis in the septic clinical context.

explanation_blueprint.title: Compensation
explanation_blueprint.body: For winter, expected PaCO2 is about 30.0 mmHg (acceptable range 28.0-32.0), and the measured value is 31.9 mmHg, so the compensation is appropriate.

explanation_blueprint.title: Anion Gap Analysis
explanation_blueprint.body: The Anion gap is 141 - (102 + 14.7) = 24.3, which is raised. A raised anion gap suggests an additional unmeasured acid load. Consider causes such as lactate, ketones, or toxins.

explanation_blueprint.title: Clinical Significance
explanation_blueprint.body: Lactic acidosis results from impaired tissue oxygen delivery or utilisation, leading to accumulation of lactate and a raised anion gap metabolic acidosis. Common causes include septic or cardiogenic shock, severe anaemia, or regional ischaemia. The elevated lactate reflects underlying hypoperfusion or cellular dysfunction. This is a marker of significant physiological stress, and urgent identification and treatment of the underlying cause is critical.

explanation_blueprint.title: Key Takeaway
explanation_blueprint.body: A raised lactate explains the gap, but the urgent job is still to find and reverse the cause of hypoperfusion or tissue stress.

explanation_blueprint.title: Diagnosis

answer_key.final_diagnosis: Respiratory alkalosis with concurrent high anion gap metabolic acidosis

cases.learning_objective: Recognise respiratory alkalosis with a concurrent high anion gap metabolic acidosis when bicarbonate is too low for isolated respiratory alkalosis

explanation_blueprint.title: pH Status
explanation_blueprint.body: The pH is 7.42, which is near normal - do not let a near-normal pH hide an underlying process.

explanation_blueprint.title: Primary Disorder
explanation_blueprint.body: PaCO2 is 19.8 mmHg, which is low and supports a primary respiratory alkalosis.

explanation_blueprint.title: Diagnosis
explanation_blueprint.body: The overall pattern is respiratory alkalosis with concurrent high anion gap metabolic acidosis.

explanation_blueprint.title: Compensation
explanation_blueprint.body: If this were isolated acute respiratory alkalosis, expected HCO3 would be about 20.0 mmol/L (acceptable range 18.0-22.0), but the measured value is 12.5 mmol/L. That mismatch does not fit a single-process compensation pattern.

explanation_blueprint.title: Anion Gap Analysis
explanation_blueprint.body: The Anion gap is 143 - (106 + 12.5) = 24.5, which is raised. A raised anion gap suggests an additional unmeasured acid load. Consider causes such as lactate, ketones, or toxins.

explanation_blueprint.title: Additional Metabolic Process
explanation_blueprint.body: There is an additional metabolic process present: High anion gap metabolic acidosis. This suggests a mixed disorder beyond simple compensation.

explanation_blueprint.title: Clinical Significance
explanation_blueprint.body: This pattern reflects significant underlying illness with two concurrent processes. A normal or near-normal pH should not reassure, as a serious metabolic acidosis may be masked by hyperventilation. This commonly occurs in conditions such as sepsis, where early respiratory changes precede clinical deterioration.

explanation_blueprint.title: Key Takeaway
explanation_blueprint.body: If a low PaCO2 does not fully explain the acid-base picture, use the anion gap to uncover an added metabolic acidosis.

cases.title: Toxic alcohol (HAGMA with appropriate respiratory compensation)
cases.clinical_stem: 34M brought to ED after possible ingestion of a suspicious substance found beside a solvent container. He is confused and reports blurred vision, with some epigastric discomfort.

cases.learning_objective: Recognise toxic alcohol ingestion as a high anion gap metabolic acidosis with appropriate respiratory compensation

explanation_blueprint.title: pH Status
explanation_blueprint.body: The pH is < 7.35, consistent with an acidaemia.

explanation_blueprint.title: Primary Disorder
explanation_blueprint.body: HCO3 is 12 mmol/L, which is low and supports a primary metabolic acidosis.

explanation_blueprint.title: Diagnosis
explanation_blueprint.body: The clinical context and only modest lactate of 2.7 mmol/L support toxic alcohol ingestion over isolated lactic acidosis.

explanation_blueprint.title: Compensation
explanation_blueprint.body: For winter, expected PaCO2 is about 26.0 mmHg (acceptable range 24.0-28.0), and the measured value is 26.9 mmHg, so the compensation is appropriate.

explanation_blueprint.title: Anion Gap Analysis
explanation_blueprint.body: The Anion gap is 141 - (98 + 12) = 31.0, which is raised. A raised anion gap suggests an additional unmeasured acid load. Consider causes such as lactate, ketones, or toxins.

explanation_blueprint.title: Clinical Significance
explanation_blueprint.body: Toxic alcohol ingestion can cause a severe raised anion gap metabolic acidosis due to accumulation of acidic metabolites. Common agents include methanol and ethylene glycol. The degree of acidosis is often disproportionate to any measured lactate, and may worsen rapidly as metabolism progresses. The key clinical clue is an unexplained high anion gap acidosis without a clear metabolic cause. This is a time-critical diagnosis, and early recognition is essential to prevent ongoing toxicity and organ injury.

explanation_blueprint.title: Key Takeaway
explanation_blueprint.body: An unexplained severe high anion gap metabolic acidosis — especially if disproportionate to lactate, should prompt urgent consideration of toxic alcohol ingestion.

cases.title: Starvation ketosis (subtle HAGMA with appropriate respiratory compensation)
cases.clinical_stem: 29M in ED with 3/7 of poor oral intake and fatigue, with recent weight loss. He looks mildly dehydrated on exam and says he’s been fasting.

questions_flow.label: Primary acid-base disorder
questions_flow.prompt: What is the primary acid-base disorder?

questions_flow.label: Anion gap
questions_flow.prompt: What is the anion gap status?

cases.learning_objective: Recognise starvation ketosis as a subtle high anion gap metabolic acidosis with appropriate respiratory compensation and normal-range glucose

explanation_blueprint.title: pH Status
explanation_blueprint.body: The pH is < 7.35, consistent with an acidaemia.

explanation_blueprint.title: Primary Disorder
explanation_blueprint.body: HCO3 is 12 mmol/L, which is low and supports a primary metabolic acidosis.

explanation_blueprint.title: Diagnosis
explanation_blueprint.body: The glucose is 4.7 mmol/L, which is not markedly elevated. This is more in keeping with starvation ketosis over DKA.

explanation_blueprint.title: Compensation
explanation_blueprint.body: For winter, expected PaCO2 is about 26.0 mmHg (acceptable range 24.0-28.0), and the measured value is 25.6 mmHg, so the compensation is appropriate.

explanation_blueprint.title: Anion Gap Analysis
explanation_blueprint.body: The Anion gap is 134 - (101 + 12) = 21.0, which is raised. A raised anion gap suggests an additional unmeasured acid load. Consider causes such as lactate, ketones, or toxins.

explanation_blueprint.title: Clinical Significance
explanation_blueprint.body: Increased ketone production in the setting of poor oral intake can cause a raised anion gap metabolic acidosis. This is typically milder than DKA because some endogenous insulin activity remains, limiting unchecked ketogenesis. The key clinical clue is a history of reduced intake with normal or only mildly elevated glucose. This pattern should prompt correction of the fasting state and assessment for the underlying reason the patient has not been eating.

explanation_blueprint.title: Key Takeaway
explanation_blueprint.body: When ketosis occurs without major hyperglycaemia, use the clinical context to separate starvation ketosis from DKA.

cases.learning_objective: Recognise starvation ketosis as a subtle high anion gap metabolic acidosis with appropriate respiratory compensation and normal-range glucose

explanation_blueprint.title: pH Status
explanation_blueprint.body: The pH is < 7.35, consistent with an acidaemia.

explanation_blueprint.title: Primary Disorder
explanation_blueprint.body: HCO3 is 17 mmol/L, which is low and supports a primary metabolic acidosis.

explanation_blueprint.title: Diagnosis
explanation_blueprint.body: The glucose is 5.9 mmol/L, which is not markedly elevated. This is more in keeping with starvation ketosis over DKA.

explanation_blueprint.title: Compensation
explanation_blueprint.body: For winter, expected PaCO2 is about 33.5 mmHg (acceptable range 31.5-35.5), and the measured value is 33.5 mmHg, so the compensation is appropriate.

explanation_blueprint.title: Anion Gap Analysis
explanation_blueprint.body: The Anion gap is 139 - (104 + 17) = 18.0, which is raised. A raised anion gap suggests an additional unmeasured acid load. Consider causes such as lactate, ketones, or toxins.

explanation_blueprint.title: Clinical Significance
explanation_blueprint.body: Increased ketone production in the setting of poor oral intake can cause a raised anion gap metabolic acidosis. This is typically milder than DKA because some endogenous insulin activity remains, limiting unchecked ketogenesis. The key clinical clue is a history of reduced intake with normal or only mildly elevated glucose. This pattern should prompt correction of the fasting state and assessment for the underlying reason the patient has not been eating.

explanation_blueprint.title: Key Takeaway
explanation_blueprint.body: When ketosis occurs without major hyperglycaemia, use the clinical context to separate starvation ketosis from DKA.

cases.learning_objective: Recognise a mixed high anion gap metabolic acidosis with concurrent metabolic alkalosis

explanation_blueprint.title: pH Status
explanation_blueprint.body: The pH is 7.36, which is near normal - do not let a near-normal pH hide an underlying process.

explanation_blueprint.title: Primary Disorder
explanation_blueprint.body: HCO3 is 24 mmol/L, which is low and supports a primary metabolic acidosis.

explanation_blueprint.title: Diagnosis
explanation_blueprint.body: The overall pattern is a mixed high anion gap metabolic acidosis with concurrent metabolic alkalosis.

explanation_blueprint.title: Compensation
explanation_blueprint.body: For winter, expected PaCO2 is about 44.0 mmHg (acceptable range 42.0-46.0), and the measured value is 43.5 mmHg, so the compensation is appropriate.

explanation_blueprint.title: Anion Gap Analysis
explanation_blueprint.body: The Anion gap is 140 - (93 + 24) = 23.0, which is raised. A raised anion gap suggests an additional unmeasured acid load. Consider causes such as lactate, ketones, or toxins.

explanation_blueprint.title: Additional Metabolic Process
explanation_blueprint.body: There is an additional metabolic process present: Metabolic alkalosis. This suggests a mixed disorder beyond simple compensation.

explanation_blueprint.title: Clinical Significance
explanation_blueprint.body: This mixed metabolic picture reflects multiple underlying processes, such as acid generation combined with vomiting or diuretic use. The presence of more than one disturbance should prompt a broader clinical search rather than a single unifying diagnosis.

explanation_blueprint.title: Key Takeaway
explanation_blueprint.body: A high anion gap acidosis with a higher-than-expected bicarbonate indicates a concurrent alkalosis. When the numbers don’t align, there is always another process.

cases.title: DKA with vomiting (mixed metabolic disorder)
cases.clinical_stem: 24-year-old man with type 1 diabetes comes to ED after a day of vomiting and abdominal discomfort. He admits he missed his insulin and is breathing deeply on arrival.

explanation_blueprint.title: pH Status
explanation_blueprint.body: The pH is < 7.35, consistent with an acidaemia.

explanation_blueprint.title: Primary Disorder
explanation_blueprint.body: HCO3 is 12 mmol/L, which is low and supports a primary metabolic acidosis.

explanation_blueprint.title: Diagnosis
explanation_blueprint.body: This fits DKA with a concurrent metabolic alkalosis from vomiting rather than an isolated ketoacidosis.

explanation_blueprint.title: Compensation
explanation_blueprint.body: For winter, expected PaCO2 is about 26.0 mmHg (acceptable range 24.0-28.0), and the measured value is 26.1 mmHg, so the compensation is appropriate.

explanation_blueprint.title: Anion Gap Analysis
explanation_blueprint.body: The Anion gap is 135 - (94 + 12) = 29.0, which is raised. A raised anion gap suggests an additional unmeasured acid load. Consider causes such as lactate, ketones, or toxins.

explanation_blueprint.title: Additional Metabolic Process
explanation_blueprint.body: There is an additional metabolic process present: Metabolic alkalosis. This suggests a mixed disorder beyond simple compensation.

explanation_blueprint.title: Clinical Significance
explanation_blueprint.body: Insulin deficiency drives ketone production, causing a raised anion gap metabolic acidosis consistent with DKA. However, concurrent vomiting leads to loss of gastric acid and a superimposed metabolic alkalosis, which can partially offset the acidaemia and result in a higher-than-expected bicarbonate. This mixed picture may make the acidosis appear less severe than expected for the degree of hyperglycaemia.

explanation_blueprint.title: Key Takeaway
explanation_blueprint.body: A preserved bicarbonate in DKA should prompt a search for a concurrent alkalosis rather than reassurance that the acidosis is mild.

cases.title: Simple normal anion gap metabolic acidosis
cases.clinical_stem: 37F in ED with several days of diarrhoea and feeling light-headed. Looks dry on exam with dry mucous membranes, ABG sent.

questions_flow.label: Primary acid-base disorder
questions_flow.prompt: What is the primary acid-base disorder?

cases.learning_objective: Recognise a simple bicarbonate-driven metabolic acidosis with a normal anion gap

explanation_blueprint.title: pH Status
explanation_blueprint.body: The pH is < 7.35, consistent with an acidaemia.

explanation_blueprint.title: Primary Disorder
explanation_blueprint.body: HCO3 is 17 mmol/L, which is low and supports a primary metabolic acidosis.

explanation_blueprint.title: Diagnosis
explanation_blueprint.body: This pattern fits gastrointestinal bicarbonate loss causing a normal anion gap metabolic acidosis.

explanation_blueprint.title: Clinical Significance
explanation_blueprint.body: A normal anion gap metabolic acidosis reflects loss of bicarbonate or impaired renal acid excretion rather than accumulation of unmeasured acids. The chloride rises to maintain electroneutrality, resulting in a hyperchloraemic acidosis. Common causes include gastrointestinal bicarbonate loss or renal tubular dysfunction.

cases.learning_objective: Recognise high anion gap metabolic acidosis with appropriate respiratory compensation

explanation_blueprint.title: pH Status
explanation_blueprint.body: The pH is < 7.35, consistent with an acidaemia.

explanation_blueprint.title: Primary Disorder
explanation_blueprint.body: HCO3 is 10 mmol/L, which is low and supports a primary metabolic acidosis.

explanation_blueprint.title: Diagnosis
explanation_blueprint.body: The glucose is 29.6 mmol/L, which strongly supports DKA as the cause of this high anion gap metabolic acidosis.

explanation_blueprint.title: Compensation
explanation_blueprint.body: For winter, expected PaCO2 is about 23.0 mmHg (acceptable range 21.0-25.0), and the measured value is 21.9 mmHg, so the compensation is appropriate.

explanation_blueprint.title: Anion Gap Analysis
explanation_blueprint.body: The Anion gap is 140 - (99 + 10) = 31.0, which is raised. A raised anion gap suggests an additional unmeasured acid load. Consider causes such as lactate, ketones, or toxins.

explanation_blueprint.title: Clinical Significance
explanation_blueprint.body: Insulin deficiency leads to unchecked lipolysis and ketone production, resulting in a raised anion gap metabolic acidosis. Marked hyperglycaemia is a key feature, distinguishing DKA from other causes of ketoacidosis. The acidosis is often more severe than in starvation or alcoholic ketoacidosis, and may be accompanied by dehydration and electrolyte disturbances.

explanation_blueprint.title: Key Takeaway
explanation_blueprint.body: High anion gap metabolic acidosis with marked hyperglycaemia suggests DKA, but always consider an additional metabolic process if compensation is inappropriate or the bicarbonate is not as expected.

cases.learning_objective: Recognise the classic respiratory alkalosis plus high anion gap metabolic acidosis pattern of salicylate toxicity

explanation_blueprint.title: pH Status
explanation_blueprint.body: The pH is > 7.45, consistent with an alkalaemia.

explanation_blueprint.title: Primary Disorder
explanation_blueprint.body: PaCO2 is 22.4 mmHg, which is low and supports a primary respiratory alkalosis.

explanation_blueprint.title: Diagnosis
explanation_blueprint.body: This is the classic salicylate pattern: respiratory alkalosis with concurrent high anion gap metabolic acidosis.

explanation_blueprint.title: Compensation
explanation_blueprint.body: If this were isolated acute respiratory alkalosis, expected HCO3 would be about 20.5 mmol/L (acceptable range 18.5-22.5), but the measured value is 16.7 mmol/L. That mismatch does not fit a single-process compensation pattern.

explanation_blueprint.title: Anion Gap Analysis
explanation_blueprint.body: The Anion gap is 136 - (101 + 16.7) = 18.3, which is raised. A raised anion gap suggests an additional unmeasured acid load. Consider causes such as lactate, ketones, or toxins.

explanation_blueprint.title: Additional Metabolic Process
explanation_blueprint.body: There is an additional metabolic process present: High anion gap metabolic acidosis. This suggests a mixed disorder beyond simple compensation.

explanation_blueprint.title: Clinical Significance
explanation_blueprint.body: This pattern is characteristic of salicylate toxicity, where early hyperventilation is followed by metabolic acidosis. The combination should prompt urgent consideration of overdose, even if the history is unclear. Early recognition is critical, as clinical deterioration can be rapid.

explanation_blueprint.title: Key Takeaway
explanation_blueprint.body: Salicylate cases are high-risk because the alkalosis and acidosis co-exist, so compensation formulas help expose the mixed picture quickly.

explanation_blueprint.body: HCO3 is 10 mmol/L, which is low and supports a primary metabolic acidosis.

explanation_blueprint.title: Diagnosis

explanation_blueprint.title: Compensation

explanation_blueprint.title: Clinical Significance
explanation_blueprint.body: Toxic alcohol ingestion can cause a severe raised anion gap metabolic acidosis due to accumulation of acidic metabolites. Common agents include methanol and ethylene glycol. The degree of acidosis is often disproportionate to any measured lactate, and may worsen rapidly as metabolism progresses. The key clinical clue is an unexplained high anion gap acidosis without a clear metabolic cause. This is a time-critical diagnosis, and early recognition is essential to prevent ongoing toxicity and organ injury.

explanation_blueprint.body: An unexplained severe high anion gap metabolic acidosis — especially if disproportionate to lactate, should prompt urgent consideration of toxic alcohol ingestion.

explanation_blueprint.title: pH Status
explanation_blueprint.body: The pH is < 7.35, consistent with an acidaemia.

explanation_blueprint.title: Primary Disorder
explanation_blueprint.body: HCO3 is 11 mmol/L, which is low and supports a primary metabolic acidosis.

explanation_blueprint.title: Diagnosis
explanation_blueprint.body: The clinical context and only modest lactate of 1.7 mmol/L support toxic alcohol ingestion over isolated lactic acidosis.

explanation_blueprint.title: Compensation
explanation_blueprint.body: For winter, expected PaCO2 is about 24.5 mmHg (acceptable range 22.5-26.5), and the measured value is 24.6 mmHg, so the compensation is appropriate.

explanation_blueprint.title: Anion Gap Analysis
explanation_blueprint.body: The Anion gap is 138 - (95 + 11) = 32.0, which is raised. A raised anion gap suggests an additional unmeasured acid load. Consider causes such as lactate, ketones, or toxins.

explanation_blueprint.title: Clinical Significance
explanation_blueprint.body: Toxic alcohol ingestion can cause a severe raised anion gap metabolic acidosis due to accumulation of acidic metabolites. Common agents include methanol and ethylene glycol. The degree of acidosis is often disproportionate to any measured lactate, and may worsen rapidly as metabolism progresses. The key clinical clue is an unexplained high anion gap acidosis without a clear metabolic cause. This is a time-critical diagnosis, and early recognition is essential to prevent ongoing toxicity and organ injury.

cases.title: Lactic acidosis (sepsis)
cases.clinical_stem: 60M brought to ED in shock with cold peripheries and mottled skin, BP low and HR up. He looks unwell and is tachypnoeic, with an ABG sent on arrival.

explanation_blueprint.title: pH Status
explanation_blueprint.body: The pH is < 7.35, consistent with an acidaemia.

explanation_blueprint.title: Primary Disorder
explanation_blueprint.body: HCO3 is 15.7 mmol/L, which is low and supports a primary metabolic acidosis.

explanation_blueprint.title: Diagnosis
explanation_blueprint.body: Lactate is 8.0 mmol/L, which supports lactic acidosis in the septic clinical context.

explanation_blueprint.title: Compensation
explanation_blueprint.body: For winter, expected PaCO2 is about 31.5 mmHg (acceptable range 29.5-33.5), and the measured value is 32.8 mmHg, so the compensation is appropriate.

explanation_blueprint.title: Anion Gap Analysis
explanation_blueprint.body: The Anion gap is 142 - (100 + 15.7) = 26.3, which is raised. A raised anion gap suggests an additional unmeasured acid load. Consider causes such as lactate, ketones, or toxins.

explanation_blueprint.title: Clinical Significance
explanation_blueprint.body: Lactic acidosis results from impaired tissue oxygen delivery or utilisation, leading to accumulation of lactate and a raised anion gap metabolic acidosis. Common causes include septic or cardiogenic shock, severe anaemia, or regional ischaemia. The elevated lactate reflects underlying hypoperfusion or cellular dysfunction. This is a marker of significant physiological stress, and urgent identification and treatment of the underlying cause is critical.

explanation_blueprint.title: Key Takeaway
explanation_blueprint.body: A raised lactate explains the gap, but the urgent job is still to find and reverse the cause of hypoperfusion or tissue stress.

explanation_blueprint.body: Opioid toxicity causes respiratory depression, leading to reduced ventilation and retention of CO₂. cases.title: Diuretic use (metabolic alkalosis with respiratory compensation)
cases.clinical_stem: 71M in ED with a few days of cramps and lightheadedness after a recent diuretic increase. He’s on loop diuretics for ankle swelling and looks a bit dry on exam.

questions_flow.label: Primary acid-base disorder
questions_flow.prompt: What is the primary acid-base disorder?

cases.learning_objective: Recognise chloride-responsive metabolic alkalosis from diuretic-associated volume depletion

explanation_blueprint.title: pH Status
explanation_blueprint.body: The pH is > 7.45, consistent with an alkalaemia.

explanation_blueprint.title: Primary Disorder
explanation_blueprint.body: HCO3 is 38 mmol/L, which is elevated and supports a primary metabolic alkalosis.

explanation_blueprint.title: Diagnosis
explanation_blueprint.body: This pattern fits chloride-responsive metabolic alkalosis. Diuretic use causes volume depletion and chloride loss, which maintains the alkalosis.

explanation_blueprint.title: Compensation
explanation_blueprint.body: For metabolic alkalosis compensation, expected PaCO2 is about 49.8 mmHg (acceptable range 46.8-52.8), and the measured value is 47.9 mmHg, so the compensation is appropriate.

explanation_blueprint.title: Clinical Significance
explanation_blueprint.body: Diuretic use can cause metabolic alkalosis through volume depletion and renal loss of chloride, leading to increased bicarbonate reabsorption. This results in a chloride-responsive metabolic alkalosis, often with low or low-normal potassium. The alkalosis is maintained by ongoing volume and chloride depletion, even after the initial trigger. Recognising this pattern is important, as correction of volume status and chloride deficit is key to reversing the disturbance.

explanation_blueprint.body: This pattern fits gastrointestinal bicarbonate loss causing a normal anion gap metabolic acidosis.

explanation_blueprint.body: For winter, expected PaCO2 is about 27.5 mmHg (acceptable range 25.5-29.5), and the measured value is 28.1 mmHg, so the compensation is appropriate.

explanation_blueprint.title: Clinical Significance
explanation_blueprint.body: Diarrhoea causes a normal anion gap metabolic acidosis through direct loss of bicarbonate from the GI tract. Recognising this mechanism helps distinguish it from renal causes of bicarbonate loss and from high anion gap acidoses.

cases.title: DKA with vomiting (mixed metabolic disorder)
cases.clinical_stem: 24-year-old man with type 1 diabetes in ED after a few episodes of vomiting and deep breathing today. He says he missed his insulin yesterday and feels generally unwell.

explanation_blueprint.body: The pH is < 7.35, consistent with an acidaemia.

explanation_blueprint.title: Primary Disorder
explanation_blueprint.body: HCO3 is 12 mmol/L, which is low and supports a primary metabolic acidosis.

explanation_blueprint.title: Diagnosis
explanation_blueprint.body: This fits DKA with a concurrent metabolic alkalosis from vomiting rather than an isolated ketoacidosis.

explanation_blueprint.title: Compensation
explanation_blueprint.body: For winter, expected PaCO2 is about 26.0 mmHg (acceptable range 24.0-28.0), and the measured value is 26.3 mmHg, so the compensation is appropriate.

explanation_blueprint.title: Anion Gap Analysis
explanation_blueprint.body: The Anion gap is 137 - (95 + 12) = 30.0, which is raised. A raised anion gap suggests an additional unmeasured acid load. Consider causes such as lactate, ketones, or toxins.

explanation_blueprint.title: Additional Metabolic Process
explanation_blueprint.body: There is an additional metabolic process present: Metabolic alkalosis. This suggests a mixed disorder beyond simple compensation.

explanation_blueprint.title: Clinical Significance
explanation_blueprint.body: Insulin deficiency drives ketone production, causing a raised anion gap metabolic acidosis consistent with DKA. However, concurrent vomiting leads to loss of gastric acid and a superimposed metabolic alkalosis, which can partially offset the acidaemia and result in a higher-than-expected bicarbonate. This mixed picture may make the acidosis appear less severe than expected for the degree of hyperglycaemia.

explanation_blueprint.title: Key Takeaway
explanation_blueprint.body: A preserved bicarbonate in DKA should prompt a search for a concurrent alkalosis rather than reassurance that the acidosis is mild.

cases.title: Alcoholic ketoacidosis (HAGMA with appropriate respiratory compensation)
cases.clinical_stem: 42M in ED after a recent alcohol binge with minimal intake for 3 days. He’s been vomiting and looks tachypnoeic on arrival.

questions_flow.label: Primary acid-base disorder
questions_flow.prompt: What is the primary acid-base disorder?

questions_flow.label: Anion gap
questions_flow.prompt: What is the anion gap status?

cases.learning_objective: Recognise alcoholic ketoacidosis as a high anion gap metabolic acidosis with appropriate respiratory compensation

explanation_blueprint.title: pH Status
explanation_blueprint.body: The pH is < 7.35, consistent with an acidaemia.

explanation_blueprint.title: Primary Disorder
explanation_blueprint.body: HCO3 is 11 mmol/L, which is low and supports a primary metabolic acidosis.

explanation_blueprint.title: Diagnosis
explanation_blueprint.body: The history pattern and the high anion gap metabolic acidosis fit alcoholic ketoacidosis.

explanation_blueprint.title: Compensation
explanation_blueprint.body: For winter, expected PaCO2 is about 24.5 mmHg (acceptable range 22.5-26.5), and the measured value is 24.1 mmHg, so the compensation is appropriate.

explanation_blueprint.title: Anion Gap Analysis
explanation_blueprint.body: The Anion gap is 133 - (95 + 11) = 27.0, which is raised. A raised anion gap suggests an additional unmeasured acid load. Consider causes such as lactate, ketones, or toxins.

explanation_blueprint.title: Clinical Significance
explanation_blueprint.body: Increased ketone production in the setting of heavy alcohol use and poor oral intake can cause a raised anion gap metabolic acidosis. Glucose is typically normal or low due to impaired gluconeogenesis, helping distinguish this from DKA. Vomiting may contribute a concurrent metabolic alkalosis, masking the degree of acidaemia. The key clue is recent alcohol use with poor intake, and management focuses on reversing the metabolic state and addressing the underlying cause.

explanation_blueprint.title: Key Takeaway
explanation_blueprint.body: A high anion gap acidosis without marked hyperglycaemia should keep alcoholic ketoacidosis high on the differential list.

cases.title: Vomiting (metabolic alkalosis with respiratory compensation)
cases.clinical_stem: 24-year-old man in ED with persistent vomiting since last night, now dry retching and unable to keep fluids down. ABG sent after ongoing symptoms.

questions_flow.label: Primary acid-base disorder
questions_flow.prompt: What is the primary acid-base disorder?

cases.learning_objective: Recognise metabolic alkalosis with appropriate respiratory compensation

explanation_blueprint.title: pH Status
explanation_blueprint.body: The pH is > 7.45, consistent with an alkalaemia.

explanation_blueprint.title: Primary Disorder
explanation_blueprint.body: HCO3 is 36 mmol/L, which is elevated and supports a primary metabolic alkalosis.

explanation_blueprint.title: Diagnosis
explanation_blueprint.body: This pattern fits chloride-responsive metabolic alkalosis from gastric losses.

explanation_blueprint.title: Compensation
explanation_blueprint.body: For metabolic alkalosis compensation, expected PaCO2 is about 48.4 mmHg (acceptable range 45.4-51.4), and the measured value is 46.9 mmHg, so the compensation is appropriate.

explanation_blueprint.title: Clinical Significance
explanation_blueprint.body: Vomiting causes loss of gastric acid, leading to a metabolic alkalosis with elevated bicarbonate and increased pH. The body compensates by hypoventilating to retain CO₂, raising PaCO₂. Ongoing chloride and volume depletion help maintain the alkalosis, so recognising this pattern is important as correction requires replacement of both volume and chloride.

cases.title: Diarrhoea (normal anion gap metabolic acidosis)
cases.clinical_stem: 29F presents with light-headedness and dehydration after several days of gastroenteritis symptoms.

questions_flow.label: Primary acid-base disorder
questions_flow.prompt: What is the primary acid-base disorder?

questions_flow.label: Anion gap

explanation_blueprint.title: pH Status
explanation_blueprint.body: The pH is < 7.35, consistent with an acidaemia.

explanation_blueprint.title: Primary Disorder
explanation_blueprint.body: HCO3 is 14 mmol/L, which is low and supports a primary metabolic acidosis.

explanation_blueprint.title: Diagnosis
explanation_blueprint.body: This pattern fits gastrointestinal bicarbonate loss causing a normal anion gap metabolic acidosis.

explanation_blueprint.title: Compensation
explanation_blueprint.body: For winter, expected PaCO2 is about 29.0 mmHg (acceptable range 27.0-31.0), and the measured value is 27.4 mmHg, so the compensation is appropriate.

explanation_blueprint.title: Anion Gap Analysis
explanation_blueprint.body: The Anion gap is 139 - (117 + 14) = 8.0, which is normal.

explanation_blueprint.title: Clinical Significance
explanation_blueprint.body: Diarrhoea causes a normal anion gap metabolic acidosis through direct loss of bicarbonate from the GI tract. This results in a hyperchloraemic acidosis as chloride rises to maintain electroneutrality. The pattern is typically accompanied by volume depletion and may be associated with low potassium. Recognising this mechanism helps distinguish it from renal causes of bicarbonate loss and from high anion gap acidoses.

explanation_blueprint.title: Key Takeaway
explanation_blueprint.body: The acidosis fits a normal anion gap pattern, but think bicarbonate loss from the gut rather than unmeasured acids.

cases.title: DKA with vomiting (mixed metabolic disorder)
cases.clinical_stem: 30-year-old woman in ED with 3/7 of vomiting and poor intake, known type 1 diabetic and says she’s missed insulin doses. On review she’s tachypnoeic with deep breathing and looks dry.

questions_flow.label: Primary acid-base disorder
questions_flow.prompt: What is the primary acid-base disorder?

questions_flow.label: Anion gap
questions_flow.prompt: What is the anion gap status?

questions_flow.label: Additional metabolic disorder
questions_flow.prompt: Is an additional metabolic disorder present?

explanation_blueprint.title: pH Status
explanation_blueprint.body: The pH is < 7.35, consistent with an acidaemia.

explanation_blueprint.title: Primary Disorder
explanation_blueprint.body: HCO3 is 14 mmol/L, which is low and supports a primary metabolic acidosis.

explanation_blueprint.title: Diagnosis
explanation_blueprint.body: This fits DKA with a concurrent metabolic alkalosis from vomiting rather than an isolated ketoacidosis.

explanation_blueprint.title: Compensation
explanation_blueprint.body: For winter, expected PaCO2 is about 29.0 mmHg (acceptable range 27.0-31.0), and the measured value is 29.8 mmHg, so the compensation is appropriate.

explanation_blueprint.title: Anion Gap Analysis
explanation_blueprint.body: The Anion gap is 134 - (93 + 14) = 27.0, which is raised. A raised anion gap suggests an additional unmeasured acid load. Consider causes such as lactate, ketones, or toxins.

explanation_blueprint.title: Additional Metabolic Process
explanation_blueprint.body: There is an additional metabolic process present: Metabolic alkalosis. This suggests a mixed disorder beyond simple compensation.

explanation_blueprint.title: Clinical Significance
explanation_blueprint.body: Insulin deficiency drives ketone production, causing a raised anion gap metabolic acidosis consistent with DKA. However, concurrent vomiting leads to loss of gastric acid and a superimposed metabolic alkalosis, which can partially offset the acidaemia and result in a higher-than-expected bicarbonate. This mixed picture may make the acidosis appear less severe than expected for the degree of hyperglycaemia.

explanation_blueprint.title: Key Takeaway
explanation_blueprint.body: A preserved bicarbonate in DKA should prompt a search for a concurrent alkalosis rather than reassurance that the acidosis is mild.

cases.learning_objective: Recognise respiratory alkalosis with a concurrent high anion gap metabolic acidosis when bicarbonate is too low for isolated respiratory alkalosis

explanation_blueprint.title: pH Status
explanation_blueprint.body: The pH is 7.36, which is near normal - do not let a near-normal pH hide an underlying process.

explanation_blueprint.title: Primary Disorder
explanation_blueprint.body: PaCO2 is 25.6 mmHg, which is low and supports a primary respiratory alkalosis.

explanation_blueprint.title: Diagnosis
explanation_blueprint.body: The overall pattern is respiratory alkalosis with concurrent high anion gap metabolic acidosis.

explanation_blueprint.title: Compensation
explanation_blueprint.body: If this were isolated acute respiratory alkalosis, expected HCO3 would be about 21.1 mmol/L (acceptable range 19.1-23.1), but the measured value is 14.0 mmol/L. That mismatch does not fit a single-process compensation pattern.

explanation_blueprint.title: Anion Gap Analysis
explanation_blueprint.body: The Anion gap is 138 - (94 + 14.0) = 30.0, which is raised. A raised anion gap suggests an additional unmeasured acid load. Consider causes such as lactate, ketones, or toxins.

explanation_blueprint.title: Additional Metabolic Process
explanation_blueprint.body: There is an additional metabolic process present: High anion gap metabolic acidosis. This suggests a mixed disorder beyond simple compensation.

explanation_blueprint.title: Clinical Significance
explanation_blueprint.body: This pattern reflects significant underlying illness with two concurrent processes. A normal or near-normal pH should not reassure, as a serious metabolic acidosis may be masked by hyperventilation. This commonly occurs in conditions such as sepsis, where early respiratory changes precede clinical deterioration.

explanation_blueprint.title: Key Takeaway
explanation_blueprint.body: If a low PaCO2 does not fully explain the acid-base picture, use the anion gap to uncover an added metabolic acidosis.

cases.title: Diuretic use (metabolic alkalosis with respiratory compensation)
cases.clinical_stem: 72M in ED with a few days of dizziness and fatigue after a recent increase in his diuretics. He looks a bit dry on exam and says he has been taking his usual tablets regularly.

questions_flow.label: Primary acid-base disorder
questions_flow.prompt: What is the primary acid-base disorder?

cases.learning_objective: Recognise chloride-responsive metabolic alkalosis from diuretic-associated volume depletion

explanation_blueprint.title: pH Status
explanation_blueprint.body: The pH is > 7.45, consistent with an alkalaemia.

explanation_blueprint.title: Primary Disorder
explanation_blueprint.body: HCO3 is 33 mmol/L, which is elevated and supports a primary metabolic alkalosis.

explanation_blueprint.title: Diagnosis
explanation_blueprint.body: This pattern fits chloride-responsive metabolic alkalosis. Diuretic use causes volume depletion and chloride loss, which maintains the alkalosis.

explanation_blueprint.title: Compensation
explanation_blueprint.body: For metabolic alkalosis compensation, expected PaCO2 is about 46.3 mmHg (acceptable range 43.3-49.3), and the measured value is 46.3 mmHg, so the compensation is appropriate.

explanation_blueprint.title: Clinical Significance
explanation_blueprint.body: Diuretic use can cause metabolic alkalosis through volume depletion and renal loss of chloride, leading to increased bicarbonate reabsorption. This results in a chloride-responsive metabolic alkalosis, often with low or low-normal potassium. The alkalosis is maintained by ongoing volume and chloride depletion, even after the initial trigger. Recognising this pattern is important, as correction of volume status and chloride deficit is key to reversing the disturbance.

Renal failure can explain a high anion gap, but compensation still needs to be checked before calling the picture isolated uraemia.

cases.title: Toxic alcohol (HAGMA with appropriate respiratory compensation)
cases.clinical_stem: 34-year-old man in ED with confusion and blurred vision after a possible ingestion from a solvent container at home. He’s tachypnoeic on arrival, with no clear history from the scene.

questions_flow.label: Primary acid-base disorder

cases.learning_objective: Recognise toxic alcohol ingestion as a high anion gap metabolic acidosis with appropriate respiratory compensation

explanation_blueprint.title: pH Status
explanation_blueprint.body: The pH is < 7.35, consistent with an acidaemia.

explanation_blueprint.title: Primary Disorder
explanation_blueprint.body: HCO3 is 13 mmol/L, which is low and supports a primary metabolic acidosis.

explanation_blueprint.title: Diagnosis
explanation_blueprint.body: The clinical context and only modest lactate of 2.7 mmol/L support toxic alcohol ingestion over isolated lactic acidosis.

explanation_blueprint.title: Compensation
explanation_blueprint.body: For winter, expected PaCO2 is about 27.5 mmHg (acceptable range 25.5-29.5), and the measured value is 28.7 mmHg, so the compensation is appropriate.

explanation_blueprint.title: Anion Gap Analysis
explanation_blueprint.body: The Anion gap is 138 - (94 + 13) = 31.0, which is raised. A raised anion gap suggests an additional unmeasured acid load. Consider causes such as lactate, ketones, or toxins.

explanation_blueprint.title: Clinical Significance
explanation_blueprint.body: Toxic alcohol ingestion can cause a severe raised anion gap metabolic acidosis due to accumulation of acidic metabolites. Common agents include methanol and ethylene glycol. The degree of acidosis is often disproportionate to any measured lactate, and may worsen rapidly as metabolism progresses. The key clinical clue is an unexplained high anion gap acidosis without a clear metabolic cause. This is a time-critical diagnosis, and early recognition is essential to prevent ongoing toxicity and organ injury.

explanation_blueprint.title: Key Takeaway
explanation_blueprint.body: An unexplained severe high anion gap metabolic acidosis — especially if disproportionate to lactate, should prompt urgent consideration of toxic alcohol ingestion.

cases.title: DKA with vomiting (mixed metabolic disorder)
cases.clinical_stem: 28F with type 1 diabetes, brought to ED after several episodes of vomiting and missed insulin yesterday. She’s tachypnoeic with deep breathing and has dry mucous membranes.

questions_flow.label: Primary acid-base disorder
questions_flow.prompt: What is the primary acid-base disorder?

questions_flow.label: Anion gap
questions_flow.prompt: What is the anion gap status?

questions_flow.label: Additional metabolic disorder
questions_flow.prompt: Is an additional metabolic disorder present?

explanation_blueprint.title: pH Status
explanation_blueprint.body: The pH is < 7.35, consistent with an acidaemia.

explanation_blueprint.title: Primary Disorder
explanation_blueprint.body: HCO3 is 13 mmol/L, which is low and supports a primary metabolic acidosis.

explanation_blueprint.title: Diagnosis
explanation_blueprint.body: This fits DKA with a concurrent metabolic alkalosis from vomiting rather than an isolated ketoacidosis.

explanation_blueprint.title: Compensation
explanation_blueprint.body: For winter, expected PaCO2 is about 27.5 mmHg (acceptable range 25.5-29.5), and the measured value is 28.5 mmHg, so the compensation is appropriate.

explanation_blueprint.title: Anion Gap Analysis
explanation_blueprint.body: The Anion gap is 136 - (92 + 13) = 31.0, which is raised. A raised anion gap suggests an additional unmeasured acid load. Consider causes such as lactate, ketones, or toxins.

explanation_blueprint.title: Additional Metabolic Process
explanation_blueprint.body: There is an additional metabolic process present: Metabolic alkalosis. This suggests a mixed disorder beyond simple compensation.

explanation_blueprint.title: Clinical Significance
explanation_blueprint.body: Insulin deficiency drives ketone production, causing a raised anion gap metabolic acidosis consistent with DKA. However, concurrent vomiting leads to loss of gastric acid and a superimposed metabolic alkalosis, which can partially offset the acidaemia and result in a higher-than-expected bicarbonate. This mixed picture may make the acidosis appear less severe than expected for the degree of hyperglycaemia.

explanation_blueprint.title: Key Takeaway
explanation_blueprint.body: A preserved bicarbonate in DKA should prompt a search for a concurrent alkalosis rather than reassurance that the acidosis is mild.

cases.title: DKA (HAGMA with appropriate respiratory compensation)
cases.clinical_stem: 23F with type 1 diabetes presents to ED after missing insulin, reporting polyuria and mild nausea. She’s tachypnoeic with deep breathing on arrival.

questions_flow.label: Primary acid-base disorder
questions_flow.prompt: What is the primary acid-base disorder?

questions_flow.label: Anion gap
questions_flow.prompt: What is the anion gap status?

cases.learning_objective: Recognise high anion gap metabolic acidosis with appropriate respiratory compensation

explanation_blueprint.title: pH Status
explanation_blueprint.body: The pH is < 7.35, consistent with an acidaemia.

explanation_blueprint.title: Primary Disorder
explanation_blueprint.body: HCO3 is 14 mmol/L, which is low and supports a primary metabolic acidosis.

explanation_blueprint.title: Diagnosis
explanation_blueprint.body: The glucose is 21.7 mmol/L, which strongly supports DKA as the cause of this high anion gap metabolic acidosis.

explanation_blueprint.title: Compensation
explanation_blueprint.body: For winter, expected PaCO2 is about 29.0 mmHg (acceptable range 27.0-31.0), and the measured value is 28.6 mmHg, so the compensation is appropriate.

explanation_blueprint.title: Anion Gap Analysis
explanation_blueprint.body: The Anion gap is 137 - (101 + 14) = 22.0, which is raised. A raised anion gap suggests an additional unmeasured acid load. Consider causes such as lactate, ketones, or toxins.

explanation_blueprint.title: Clinical Significance
explanation_blueprint.body: Insulin deficiency leads to unchecked lipolysis and ketone production, resulting in a raised anion gap metabolic acidosis. Marked hyperglycaemia is a key feature, distinguishing DKA from other causes of ketoacidosis. The acidosis is often more severe than in starvation or alcoholic ketoacidosis, and may be accompanied by dehydration and electrolyte disturbances.

explanation_blueprint.title: Key Takeaway
explanation_blueprint.body: High anion gap metabolic acidosis with marked hyperglycaemia suggests DKA, but always consider an additional metabolic process if compensation is inappropriate or the bicarbonate is not as expected.

answer_key.final_diagnosis: Respiratory acidosis with concurrent high anion gap metabolic acidosis

cases.learning_objective: Recognise respiratory acidosis with a concurrent high anion gap metabolic acidosis when bicarbonate is too low for isolated respiratory compensation

explanation_blueprint.title: pH Status
explanation_blueprint.body: The pH is < 7.35, consistent with an acidaemia.

explanation_blueprint.title: Primary Disorder
explanation_blueprint.body: PaCO2 is 63.6 mmHg, which is elevated and supports a primary respiratory acidosis.

explanation_blueprint.title: Diagnosis
explanation_blueprint.body: The overall pattern is respiratory acidosis with concurrent high anion gap metabolic acidosis.

explanation_blueprint.title: Compensation
explanation_blueprint.body: If this were isolated chronic respiratory acidosis, expected HCO3 would be about 33.4 mmol/L (acceptable range 31.4-35.4), but the measured value is 31.0 mmol/L. That mismatch does not fit a single-process compensation pattern.

explanation_blueprint.title: Anion Gap Analysis
explanation_blueprint.body: The Anion gap is 142 - (92 + 31.0) = 19.0, which is raised. A raised anion gap suggests an additional unmeasured acid load. Consider causes such as lactate, ketones, or toxins.

explanation_blueprint.title: Additional Metabolic Process
explanation_blueprint.body: There is an additional metabolic process present: High anion gap metabolic acidosis. This suggests a mixed disorder beyond simple compensation.

explanation_blueprint.title: Clinical Significance
explanation_blueprint.body: This pattern suggests severe physiological compromise, with both impaired ventilation and a concurrent metabolic acidosis. It is often seen in critically unwell patients and indicates a high risk of deterioration. Prompt assessment and escalation of care are essential.

explanation_blueprint.title: Key Takeaway
explanation_blueprint.body: When respiratory acidosis and a raised anion gap coexist, expected compensation helps confirm that two primary processes are present.

explanation_blueprint.title: pH Status
explanation_blueprint.body: The pH is < 7.35, consistent with an acidaemia.

explanation_blueprint.title: Primary Disorder
explanation_blueprint.body: HCO3 is 17 mmol/L, which is low and supports a primary metabolic acidosis.

explanation_blueprint.title: Diagnosis
explanation_blueprint.body: The glucose is 5.5 mmol/L, which is not markedly elevated. This is more in keeping with starvation ketosis over DKA.

explanation_blueprint.title: Compensation
explanation_blueprint.body: For winter, expected PaCO2 is about 33.5 mmHg (acceptable range 31.5-35.5), and the measured value is 32.3 mmHg, so the compensation is appropriate.

explanation_blueprint.title: Anion Gap Analysis
explanation_blueprint.body: The Anion gap is 138 - (103 + 17) = 18.0, which is raised. A raised anion gap suggests an additional unmeasured acid load. Consider causes such as lactate, ketones, or toxins.

explanation_blueprint.title: Clinical Significance
explanation_blueprint.body: Increased ketone production in the setting of poor oral intake can cause a raised anion gap metabolic acidosis. This is typically milder than DKA because some endogenous insulin activity remains, limiting unchecked ketogenesis. The key clinical clue is a history of reduced intake with normal or only mildly elevated glucose. This pattern should prompt correction of the fasting state and assessment for the underlying reason the patient has not been eating.

explanation_blueprint.title: Key Takeaway
explanation_blueprint.body: When ketosis occurs without major hyperglycaemia, use the clinical context to separate starvation ketosis from DKA.

cases.title: Vomiting (metabolic alkalosis with respiratory compensation)
cases.clinical_stem: 24-year-old man in ED with persistent vomiting for the last day and a bit of retching overnight. He looks dry with dry mucous membranes and is struggling to keep fluids down.

questions_flow.label: Primary acid-base disorder
questions_flow.prompt: What is the primary acid-base disorder?

cases.learning_objective: Recognise metabolic alkalosis with appropriate respiratory compensation

explanation_blueprint.title: pH Status
explanation_blueprint.body: The pH is > 7.45, consistent with an alkalaemia.

explanation_blueprint.title: Primary Disorder
explanation_blueprint.body: HCO3 is 33 mmol/L, which is elevated and supports a primary metabolic alkalosis.

explanation_blueprint.title: Diagnosis
explanation_blueprint.body: This pattern fits chloride-responsive metabolic alkalosis from gastric losses.

explanation_blueprint.title: Compensation
explanation_blueprint.body: For metabolic alkalosis compensation, expected PaCO2 is about 46.3 mmHg (acceptable range 43.3-49.3), and the measured value is 44.6 mmHg, so the compensation is appropriate.

explanation_blueprint.title: Clinical Significance
explanation_blueprint.body: Vomiting causes loss of gastric acid, leading to a metabolic alkalosis with elevated bicarbonate and increased pH. The body compensates by hypoventilating to retain CO₂, raising PaCO₂. Ongoing chloride and volume depletion help maintain the alkalosis, so recognising this pattern is important as correction requires replacement of both volume and chloride.

explanation_blueprint.title: pH Status

explanation_blueprint.title: Diagnosis
explanation_blueprint.body: This pattern fits opioid-related hypoventilation.

explanation_blueprint.title: Clinical Significance
explanation_blueprint.body: Opioid toxicity causes respiratory depression, leading to reduced ventilation and retention of CO₂. This results in a respiratory acidosis with an elevated PaCO₂ and a fall in pH. The key problem is inadequate ventilation, and early recognition is important as this is a reversible cause of respiratory failure.

cases.learning_objective: Recognise a mixed high anion gap metabolic acidosis with concurrent metabolic alkalosis

explanation_blueprint.title: pH Status
explanation_blueprint.body: The pH is 7.35, which is near normal - do not let a near-normal pH hide an underlying process.

explanation_blueprint.title: Primary Disorder
explanation_blueprint.body: HCO3 is 20 mmol/L, which is low and supports a primary metabolic acidosis.

explanation_blueprint.title: Diagnosis
explanation_blueprint.body: The overall pattern is a mixed high anion gap metabolic acidosis with concurrent metabolic alkalosis.

explanation_blueprint.title: Compensation
explanation_blueprint.body: For winter, expected PaCO2 is about 38.0 mmHg (acceptable range 36.0-40.0), and the measured value is 37.9 mmHg, so the compensation is appropriate.

explanation_blueprint.title: Anion Gap Analysis
explanation_blueprint.body: The Anion gap is 141 - (95 + 20) = 26.0, which is raised. A raised anion gap suggests an additional unmeasured acid load. Consider causes such as lactate, ketones, or toxins.

explanation_blueprint.title: Additional Metabolic Process
explanation_blueprint.body: There is an additional metabolic process present: Metabolic alkalosis. This suggests a mixed disorder beyond simple compensation.

explanation_blueprint.title: Clinical Significance
explanation_blueprint.body: This mixed metabolic picture reflects multiple underlying processes, such as acid generation combined with vomiting or diuretic use. The presence of more than one disturbance should prompt a broader clinical search rather than a single unifying diagnosis.

explanation_blueprint.title: Key Takeaway
explanation_blueprint.body: A high anion gap acidosis with a higher-than-expected bicarbonate indicates a concurrent alkalosis. When the numbers don’t align, there is always another process.

cases.title: Alcoholic ketoacidosis (HAGMA with appropriate respiratory compensation)
cases.clinical_stem: 31F presents to ED with nausea and vomiting after a recent alcohol binge. She reports poor oral intake over the past couple of days and has had epigastric discomfort.

cases.learning_objective: Recognise alcoholic ketoacidosis as a high anion gap metabolic acidosis with appropriate respiratory compensation

explanation_blueprint.title: pH Status
explanation_blueprint.body: The pH is < 7.35, consistent with an acidaemia.

explanation_blueprint.title: Primary Disorder
explanation_blueprint.body: HCO3 is 17 mmol/L, which is low and supports a primary metabolic acidosis.

explanation_blueprint.title: Diagnosis
explanation_blueprint.body: The history pattern and the high anion gap metabolic acidosis fit alcoholic ketoacidosis.

explanation_blueprint.title: Compensation
explanation_blueprint.body: For winter, expected PaCO2 is about 33.5 mmHg (acceptable range 31.5-35.5), and the measured value is 34.5 mmHg, so the compensation is appropriate.

explanation_blueprint.title: Anion Gap Analysis
explanation_blueprint.body: The Anion gap is 134 - (99 + 17) = 18.0, which is raised. A raised anion gap suggests an additional unmeasured acid load. Consider causes such as lactate, ketones, or toxins.

explanation_blueprint.title: Clinical Significance
explanation_blueprint.body: Increased ketone production in the setting of heavy alcohol use and poor oral intake can cause a raised anion gap metabolic acidosis. Glucose is typically normal or low due to impaired gluconeogenesis, helping distinguish this from DKA. Vomiting may contribute a concurrent metabolic alkalosis, masking the degree of acidaemia. The key clue is recent alcohol use with poor intake, and management focuses on reversing the metabolic state and addressing the underlying cause.

explanation_blueprint.title: Key Takeaway
explanation_blueprint.body: A high anion gap acidosis without marked hyperglycaemia should keep alcoholic ketoacidosis high on the differential list.

cases.title: Diarrhoea (normal anion gap metabolic acidosis)
cases.clinical_stem: 34F presents dehydrated and weak after several days of high-output gastrointestinal losses.

questions_flow.prompt: What is the primary acid-base disorder?

cases.learning_objective: Recognise normal anion gap metabolic acidosis with appropriate respiratory compensation

explanation_blueprint.title: pH Status
explanation_blueprint.body: The pH is < 7.35, consistent with an acidaemia.

explanation_blueprint.title: Primary Disorder
explanation_blueprint.body: HCO3 is 13 mmol/L, which is low and supports a primary metabolic acidosis.

explanation_blueprint.title: Diagnosis
explanation_blueprint.body: This pattern fits gastrointestinal bicarbonate loss causing a normal anion gap metabolic acidosis.

explanation_blueprint.title: Compensation
explanation_blueprint.body: For winter, expected PaCO2 is about 27.5 mmHg (acceptable range 25.5-29.5), and the measured value is 25.8 mmHg, so the compensation is appropriate.

explanation_blueprint.title: Anion Gap Analysis
explanation_blueprint.body: The Anion gap is 137 - (115 + 13) = 9.0, which is normal.

explanation_blueprint.title: Clinical Significance
explanation_blueprint.body: Diarrhoea causes a normal anion gap metabolic acidosis through direct loss of bicarbonate from the GI tract. This results in a hyperchloraemic acidosis as chloride rises to maintain electroneutrality. The pattern is typically accompanied by volume depletion and may be associated with low potassium. Recognising this mechanism helps distinguish it from renal causes of bicarbonate loss and from high anion gap acidoses.

explanation_blueprint.title: Key Takeaway
explanation_blueprint.body: The acidosis fits a normal anion gap pattern, but think bicarbonate loss from the gut rather than unmeasured acids.

cases.learning_objective: Recognise starvation ketosis as a subtle high anion gap metabolic acidosis with appropriate respiratory compensation and normal-range glucose

explanation_blueprint.title: pH Status
explanation_blueprint.body: The pH is < 7.35, consistent with an acidaemia.

explanation_blueprint.title: Primary Disorder
explanation_blueprint.body: HCO3 is 16 mmol/L, which is low and supports a primary metabolic acidosis.

explanation_blueprint.title: Diagnosis
explanation_blueprint.body: The glucose is 3.3 mmol/L, which is not markedly elevated. This is more in keeping with starvation ketosis over DKA.

explanation_blueprint.title: Compensation
explanation_blueprint.body: For winter, expected PaCO2 is about 32.0 mmHg (acceptable range 30.0-34.0), and the measured value is 33.4 mmHg, so the compensation is appropriate.

explanation_blueprint.title: Anion Gap Analysis
explanation_blueprint.body: The Anion gap is 137 - (103 + 16) = 18.0, which is raised. A raised anion gap suggests an additional unmeasured acid load. Consider causes such as lactate, ketones, or toxins.

explanation_blueprint.title: Clinical Significance
explanation_blueprint.body: Increased ketone production in the setting of poor oral intake can cause a raised anion gap metabolic acidosis. This is typically milder than DKA because some endogenous insulin activity remains, limiting unchecked ketogenesis. The key clinical clue is a history of reduced intake with normal or only mildly elevated glucose. This pattern should prompt correction of the fasting state and assessment for the underlying reason the patient has not been eating.

explanation_blueprint.title: Key Takeaway
explanation_blueprint.body: When ketosis occurs without major hyperglycaemia, use the clinical context to separate starvation ketosis from DKA.

explanation_blueprint.title: pH Status
explanation_blueprint.body: The pH is < 7.35, consistent with an acidaemia.

explanation_blueprint.body: PaCO2 is 59.9 mmHg, which is elevated and supports a primary respiratory acidosis.

explanation_blueprint.title: Compensation
explanation_blueprint.body: If this were isolated acute respiratory acidosis, expected HCO3 would be about 26.0 mmol/L (acceptable range 24.0-28.0), but the measured value is 21.0 mmol/L. That mismatch does not fit a single-process compensation pattern.

explanation_blueprint.title: Anion Gap Analysis
explanation_blueprint.body: The Anion gap is 142 - (97 + 21.0) = 24.0, which is raised. A raised anion gap suggests an additional unmeasured acid load. Consider causes such as lactate, ketones, or toxins.

explanation_blueprint.title: Clinical Significance
explanation_blueprint.body: This pattern suggests severe physiological compromise, with both impaired ventilation and a concurrent metabolic acidosis. It is often seen in critically unwell patients and indicates a high risk of deterioration. Prompt assessment and escalation of care are essential.

explanation_blueprint.body: When respiratory acidosis and a raised anion gap coexist, expected compensation helps confirm that two primary processes are present.

explanation_blueprint.body: The history pattern and the high anion gap metabolic acidosis fit alcoholic ketoacidosis.

explanation_blueprint.title: Compensation
explanation_blueprint.body: For winter, expected PaCO2 is about 30.5 mmHg (acceptable range 28.5-32.5), and the measured value is 31.7 mmHg, so the compensation is appropriate.

explanation_blueprint.title: Anion Gap Analysis
explanation_blueprint.body: The Anion gap is 132 - (99 + 15) = 18.0, which is raised. A raised anion gap suggests an additional unmeasured acid load. Consider causes such as lactate, ketones, or toxins.

explanation_blueprint.title: Clinical Significance
explanation_blueprint.body: Increased ketone production in the setting of heavy alcohol use and poor oral intake can cause a raised anion gap metabolic acidosis. Glucose is typically normal or low due to impaired gluconeogenesis, helping distinguish this from DKA. Vomiting may contribute a concurrent metabolic alkalosis, masking the degree of acidaemia. The key clue is recent alcohol use with poor intake, and management focuses on reversing the metabolic state and addressing the underlying cause.

explanation_blueprint.title: Key Takeaway
explanation_blueprint.body: A high anion gap acidosis without marked hyperglycaemia should keep alcoholic ketoacidosis high on the differential list.

cases.title: Simple respiratory alkalosis
cases.clinical_stem: 33F in ED with anxiety and rapid breathing, RR high on arrival. She looks distressed but otherwise settles between episodes.

questions_flow.label: Primary acid-base disorder
questions_flow.prompt: What is the primary acid-base disorder?

cases.learning_objective: Recognise acute respiratory alkalosis with appropriate metabolic compensation

explanation_blueprint.title: pH Status
explanation_blueprint.body: The pH is > 7.45, consistent with an alkalaemia.

explanation_blueprint.title: Primary Disorder
explanation_blueprint.body: PaCO2 is 29 mmHg, which is low and supports a primary respiratory alkalosis.

explanation_blueprint.title: Diagnosis
explanation_blueprint.body: This pattern fits simple hyperventilation.

explanation_blueprint.title: Clinical Significance
explanation_blueprint.body: Respiratory alkalosis is caused by hyperventilation, leading to excessive loss of CO₂ and a rise in pH. This results in a low PaCO₂ with a compensatory fall in bicarbonate over time. Common causes include pain, anxiety, hypoxia, or early illness.

cases.title: COPD exacerbation (acute-on-chronic respiratory acidosis)
cases.clinical_stem: 68M with known COPD and a long smoking history presents with worsening breathlessness over the past few days. He’s been coughing up more sputum than usual and is working harder to breathe in ED.

questions_flow.label: Primary acid-base disorder
questions_flow.prompt: What is the primary acid-base disorder?

explanation_blueprint.title: pH Status
explanation_blueprint.body: The pH is < 7.35, consistent with an acidaemia.

explanation_blueprint.title: Primary Disorder
explanation_blueprint.body: PaCO2 is 71.0 mmHg, which is elevated and supports a primary respiratory acidosis.

explanation_blueprint.title: Diagnosis
explanation_blueprint.body: This pattern is consistent with an acute on chronic respiratory acidosis. Chronic CO2 retention leads to metabolic compensation, with an acute rise in the PaCO2 during exacerbation.

explanation_blueprint.title: Compensation
explanation_blueprint.body: The measured compensatory response does not fit the expected range for chronic respiratory acidosis, so the compensation is inappropriate.

explanation_blueprint.title: Clinical Significance
explanation_blueprint.body: An acute COPD exacerbation causes worsening ventilatory failure, leading to a rise in PaCO₂ and a fall in pH. Renal compensation has not had time to adjust, so the bicarbonate does not increase appropriately. This represents an acute deterioration on a background of chronic respiratory disease.
